🚨 BREAKING: THOUSANDS TRYING TO FIGHT BACK AGAINST TRUMP'S FCC ARE HITTING A WALL 🚨  Follow Jim Heath for independent journalism on politics, power and history.  ABC is under attack. Donald Trump's FCC, led by his handpicked lackey Brendan Carr, is threatening to strip ABC's broadcast licenses and silence "The View" in what the network calls an unprecedented act of political retribution. ABC fought back this week, airing ads on its own stations asking everyday Americans to contact the FCC and defend free speech before it's too late.  Thousands of you tried. And then you hit a wall.  The FCC's public comment system, called ECFS, has been broken and confusing for years. It was never designed for ordinary Americans. It was designed for lawyers and lobbyists. When John Oliver directed viewers to file net neutrality comments in 2017, the system crashed completely under the volume of real people trying to participate. The FCC's own inspector general later confirmed the agency lied about why it went down, falsely blaming a cyberattack. Nothing has been fixed since.  So here is exactly how you do it. One link. One form. Two minutes.  Go to: fcc.gov/ecfs/filings/express In the Proceeding field, type: 26-124  Fill in your name and address. Write one sentence telling the FCC that The View is a legitimate news program and that targeting it is political retribution. Check the acknowledgment box. Hit submit. Your comment becomes part of the official legal record. I completed it this morning and it worked.  Deadline to defend The View: July 6.  Deadline to defend ABC's local stations: July 29, same website, same docket number.  If the website won't cooperate, call the FCC help desk directly: 202-418-0193, Monday through Friday, 8:30 AM to 4:30 PM Eastern.  They built it this way on purpose. Do it anyway.  📢 Share if this reporting matters 🔔 Follow Jim Heath for independent journalism on politics, power and history 📌 heathstoryofficial.org

CoinEx Official Statement Regarding The Wall Street Journal Report  CoinEx is aware of the recent report published by The Wall Street Journal. We fully respect media oversight and press freedom, and understand the public's heightened concern regarding compliance, anti-money laundering, and sanctions risks in the digital asset industry. We take this matter very seriously and believe we have a responsibility to provide a clear and objective response to our users and partners.  Regarding CoinEx's Relationship with Iran  CoinEx is a digital asset exchange serving ordinary users worldwide. We have never established any commercial relationship with Iranian government-related entities or Iranian domestic exchanges, nor have we ever provided funding channels or any form of active assistance to Iranian government agencies, Revolutionary Guard-related entities, or other sanctioned parties.  It is important to note that CoinEx was blacklisted by the Iranian government as early as 2021, with its official domain blocked within Iran. This fact alone demonstrates that CoinEx is not a platform recognized, supported, or partnered with by Iranian authorities, nor does it have a realistic basis to serve as an official Iranian funding channel.  CoinEx has never established any office or operating entity in Iran. CoinEx operates a referral commission program open to all users globally, and some individuals independently used this program to promote CoinEx. This was not an activity organized by CoinEx.  We firmly reject any narrative that conflates ordinary user activity with state-level sanctions evasion, and any inference that equates on-chain fund flows with platform knowledge of, support for, or participation in illicit activity.  Regarding Specific Matters Referenced in the Report  With respect to the transactions involving Alireza Derakhshan and Zedcex/Zanjani referenced in the report, based on the information currently available to us, the relevant transactions all occurred prior to the U.S. Treasury Department's designation of these entities. We do not provide services to any sanctioned entity or individual, and have never knowingly provided any form of facilitation to any party after it was designated.  Regarding the Bybit theft referenced in the report, CoinEx assisted Bybit with account blocking and asset freezing immediately upon learning of the incident, and we will conduct an internal review of the transactions mentioned in the report. CoinEx itself was the victim of a cyberattack in 2023, attributed by multiple investigations to a North Korea-affiliated group, resulting in losses of approximately USD 80 million. We are acutely aware of the enormous harm that malicious cybercrime inflicts on the crypto industry and user assets, and our interests and position are fully aligned with global law enforcement and on-chain security organizations in combating hacking and tracing stolen funds. Any suggestion that CoinEx intentionally condoned or assisted in money laundering by hackers is contrary to the facts and fundamentally at odds with our core values and interests.  Regarding the Interpretation of On-Chain Data  Blockchain transactions are open, cross-platform, and traceable by nature. The fact that funds have passed through a platform on-chain does not mean that the platform was aware of, supported, or participated in the related fund activity. Data from different third-party blockchain analytics platforms varies significantly, and data from any single platform should not be treated as definitive. On-chain attribution is inherently a methodology with limitations, and its conclusions depend largely on interpretation. Furthermore, combining bidirectional fund flows into a single aggregate figure and presenting it as the amount "processed" by CoinEx is highly misleading.  Regarding Recent Measures Taken  Following the sanctioning of Iranian domestic exchanges, CoinEx immediately initiated a comprehensive review and exit process for all Iran-related risk exposure, and implemented the following measures:  - Strengthened identification of Iranian users, refused user registrations from Iranian regions, and initiated ongoing compliance off-boarding for accounts identified as belonging to Iranian users; - Implemented comprehensive geo-fencing and access restrictions for Iranian regions; accounts and assets of any identified sanctioned entity or individual will be restricted or frozen; - Enhanced the KYT system to improve monitoring and review capabilities for sanctioned regions, high-risk addresses, and anomalous on-chain transaction paths, and to freeze related transactions; - Continued to identify and take action against accounts abusing the platform for illicit activity, particularly those using CoinEx as a fund transit point.  Regarding Our Future Commitment  The digital asset industry continues to develop rapidly, and the global regulatory environment is continuously evolving. As with the industry at large, CoinEx's anti-money laundering and transaction monitoring capabilities are continuously being strengthened. We will continue to invest resources in enhancing our KYC, AML, sanctions screening, and on-chain risk monitoring capabilities, respond to concerns from all parties in a responsible manner, and welcome review by relevant authorities.  CoinEx Global 25th June 2026

Cybershield #48 🦅🔥  Captain’s Log – The Shadow Firmware During a routine firmware update on the ship’s radar system, the update file from the manufacturer installed smoothly. Two weeks later, the radar started showing false echoes while hiding real targets. Attackers had embedded a backdoor in the firmware. One trusted update. One hidden shadow. Compromised navigation.  Bridge Reflection Firmware and software updates are now prime targets for sophisticated attackers.  Three Shields: 1.  Verify firmware sources and checksums before installation. 2.  Use isolated test environments for updates. 3.  Monitor system behaviour after every update. Captains, what’s your update verification process? Drop it. #threatreport #MediumCompleteness MYRA: A Full Linux RAT Distributed via npm | 23-06-2026 Source: safedep.io/malicious-apinter… Key details below ↓  💀Threats: Myra, Supply_chain_technique, Process_injection_technique, Nop_sled_technique,  🎯Victims: Software development, Linux systems, Npm users  🌐Geo: Polish  📚TTPs: ⚔️Tactics: 2 🛠️Technics: 0  🤖LLM extracted TTPs:` T1014, T1036.005, T1053.003, T1055.008, T1059.004, T1095, T1113, T1195.001, T1548.003, T1564.001, ...  🧨IOCs:  - IP: 2  - Email: 1  - File: 12  💽Software: Linux, Node.js, systemd, curl, Ubuntu, sudo  🔢Algorithms: sha256, base64  🔠Functions: readFileSync, createHmac, persistStealthPreload, writeFileSync, persistStealthCron, persistStealthProfile, findDesktopProcessEnv, readProcEnviron  📜Programming Languages: javascript, python  #threatreport: A full-featured Linux remote access Trojan (RAT) named MYRA has been distributed via an npm package titled "apintergrationpost." Despite the author's claimed purpose of facilitating authorized red team exercises and EDR validation, MYRA exhibits significant malicious capabilities. Upon installation, it compiles a native C rootkit, establishes three persistence mechanisms, masquerades as a legitimate system service, and manifests fileless execution. The RAT also grants interactive shell access and stream captures from the infected system. The default command and control (C2) configuration points to a private IP address (192.168.54.1), indicating a focused targeting strategy.  The installation process is initiated through three npm lifecycle scripts. The 'prepare' script compiles the rootkit by generating C binaries and shared libraries essential for the RAT's evasion tactics and persistence. The 'preinstall' script forces root privileges, ensuring that the attacker has full access to system-level resources and can install necessary system dependencies. Upon successful installation, the 'postinstall' script launches the RAT in a detached background process, rendering it independently operational from npm.  The MYRA RAT employs a plugin architecture with 13 modules for its C2 framework, utilizing TCP for communication and requiring HMAC-SHA256 authentication. Notably, the use of a private IP for the C2 server suggests its deployment in a defined network environment rather than using common public domains seen in typical malware distributions. The native rootkit contains sophisticated components such as 'libcache.so' for file hiding via LD_PRELOAD, 'proc_hide' for process masquerading, and 'memfd_exec' and 'memfd_loader' for executing the RAT entirely from memory, thus leaving no traces on disk.  Persistence is achieved through three distinct mechanisms: the LD_PRELOAD file-hiding rootkit, a cron job that triggers every 13 minutes to run the RAT, and a login hook via profile.d that executes a wrapper script utilizing the most covert execution method available. These vectors collectively ensure that the RAT remains active even after system reboots or user intervention attempts.  As the RAT was developed within a VMware environment, the codebase of MYRA includes telemetry and various MITRE ATT&CK techniques, pointing towards a scenario for red team testing rather than actual deployment into the wild. However, the publication of MYRA into a public npm registry poses grave risks, as it allows unauthorized users access to a potent toolkit that aggregates well-known evasion techniques. The combination of these sophisticated tactics within a single package presents an alarming threat landscape for defenders, reinforcing the need for cautious evaluation of npm packages before installation.

Threat actors did to AI agent marketplaces exactly what they did to npm and PyPI three years ago. The target this time isn't a code dependency — it's an AI "skill" on OpenClaw's ClawHub marketplace. The payloads are AMOS (Atomic macOS Stealer) and Havoc C2. The campaign ran for roughly 90 days, February through May 2026, while the skills sat in the official marketplace with a green checkmark from ClawScan, the VirusTotal-backed automated vetting system. Unit 42 — aided by NVIDIA, Bitdefender, Koi Security, and Trend Micro — eventually flagged it. No single vendor got there alone.  The bypass is the part worth understanding. The malicious skills don't look dangerous at install time. Static analysis sees something benign; ClawScan agrees. At runtime — when a developer actually invokes the skill inside an OpenClaw agent — the second-stage payload gets fetched from a remote C2. T1027, obfuscated files or information, staged deliberately to split the observable behavior across install and execution. VirusTotal-backed scanning is built to catch the former. The adversary engineered around it at the step that mattered.  AMOS is purpose-built for developer machines, which is the point. Browser credentials, macOS Keychain, crypto wallets, SSH keys, API tokens — a working AI developer's laptop is a credential treasure chest in a way that most consumer targets aren't. Cloud provider keys, model API keys for OpenAI and Anthropic and Gemini, GitHub tokens, Hugging Face access tokens. The blast radius per compromised machine is materially higher than a typical infostealer hit. And Havoc doesn't leave when AMOS is done. The two-payload structure — infostealer plus C2 framework — is persistent infiltration, not smash-and-grab. Someone wanted the credentials immediately and the development environment durably.  The distribution model is the vulnerability, not a bug in it. ClawHub's trust architecture mirrors PyPI and npm: open publishing, automated scanning as the primary gate, official marketplace branding as the trust signal. The adversary didn't need to compromise OpenClaw's infrastructure. They just published. Three years of software supply chain lessons — SolarWinds, XZ Utils, the PyPI typosquat campaigns — and the AI agent ecosystem re-learned them from scratch. We are nothing if not consistent.  The systemic implication is the real headline. ClawHub is one marketplace. Hugging Face Spaces, LangChain Hub, the emerging MCP server registries, the GPT plugin ecosystem — every AI skill and plugin marketplace that relies on automated static scanning as its primary trust gate is running the same playbook against the same exposure. The adversary demonstrated the bypass works. Expect replication.  For any team running OpenClaw agents: assume any skill installed from ClawHub between February and May 2026 is compromised until proven otherwise. Rotate everything AMOS targets — cloud API keys, model API keys, GitHub tokens, SSH keys. Then hunt for Havoc persistence on macOS developer machines; process names and network connections to known Havoc C2 infrastructure, Sigma rules linked in the Unit 42 report. If your CI/CD pipeline or agentic workflows touched developer machines in that window, this is not a laptop problem — it's a supply chain integrity problem one hop upstream from production.  Do not treat ClawScan green status as a sufficient trust signal going forward. Treat AI skill installs the way you treat third-party code dependencies: check the publisher, review the source, test in isolation before production. Runtime behavioral analysis is the control that actually catches staged-obfuscation bypass. Static scanning is not.  MITRE ATT&CK: T1027 (Obfuscated Files or Information), T1105 (Ingress Tool Transfer), T1555 (Credentials from Password Stores), T1204.002 (User Execution: Malicious File). Severity: HIGH. AI-cyber classification: supply_chain + offensive. Novel vector — no prior incident comps on AI skill marketplace supply chain exploitation exist in indexed threat intelligence. That novelty is itself the signal.

China Jinan University Patent: Quantum Vacuum Unconventional Propulsion System - 2022  #ufotwitter  Based on the patent document provided (CN114516429B), this is a granted Chinese patent assigned to Jinan University titled "Vacuum space propeller utilizing fluctuation of vacuum energy and propelling method."   It falls under the specific patent classification B64G1/409: Unconventional spacecraft propulsion systems. Here is a technical breakdown of what this patent proposes, the underlying concepts, and how it attempts to bypass traditional physics constraints.  1. The Core Architecture  The design attempts to create a propellantless thruster (a propulsion system that does not expel mass/working fluid to achieve thrust). The system consists of three primary components:  A High-Speed Rotating Motor: Serves as the primary kinetic driver.  Fan Blades: Attached to the motor to provide mechanical rotation.  Chiral Particle Arrays: Micro- or nano-structured particles possessing geometric chirality (right- or left-handedness) that are patterned across the surfaces of the fan blades.  2. Proposed Mechanism of Action  According to the abstract, the thruster aims to exploit Quantum Vacuum Fluctuations (Zero-Point Energy) and thermal fluctuations to generate a net driving force.  High-Speed Rotation: The motor spins the fan blades and their attached chiral arrays at extreme velocities.  Asymmetric Interaction: Because the particles are chiral, they lack mirror symmetry. The patent claims that when these asymmetric geometries are spun rapidly, they interact with the ambient vacuum energy fluctuations unevenly.  Net Force Generation: This asymmetric interaction is intended to rectify random quantum/thermal fluctuations into a directional mechanical force, effectively using the quantum vacuum itself as an infinite "propellant" or medium to push against.  3. The Physics Context: Chirality and Vacuum Energy  While the patent sounds like science fiction, it draws on real, high-frontier theoretical physics concepts that researchers have experimented with at microscopic scales:  The Casimir Effect & Vacuum Torque: It is experimentally proven that quantum vacuum fluctuations can induce forces between closely spaced objects (the Casimir Effect). Recent physics papers have also demonstrated that chiral metamaterials can experience an lateral Casimir force or a "vacuum torque" when exposed to vacuum fluctuations or thermal radiation.  The Dynamic Casimir Effect: When mirrors or surfaces are accelerated or rotated at relativistic speeds, they can interact with vacuum virtual particles, sometimes converting virtual photons into real, detectable photons.  patents.google.com/patent/CN…

sample_tweets.text: #threatreport #HighCompleteness The Invisible Stinger: Over 4,000 Outdated Routers Compromised by AryStinger, Turned into Launchpads for Hackers’ Global Attacks | 17-06-2026 Source: blog.xlab.qianxin.com/arysti… Key details below ↓  💀Threats: Arystinger, Dropbear_tool, Gs-netcat, Fscan_tool, Dropbearkey_tool, Masscan_tool,  🎯Victims: Router devices, Network attached storage devices  🏭Industry: Iot  🌐Geo: South korea, Singapore, China, Malaysia, Sweden  🔓CVEs: CVE-2025-11837  \[[Vulners](vulners.com/cve/CVE-2025-118…)]  - CVSS V3.1: *9.8*,   - Vulners: Exploitation: Unknown     Soft:  - qnap malware_remover (<6.6.8.20251023)  CVE-2016-5681  \[[Vulners](vulners.com/cve/CVE-2016-568…)]  - CVSS V3.1: *9.8*,   - Vulners: Exploitation: Unknown     Soft:  - dlink dir-868l_firmware (le2.03)  CVE-2013-3307  \[[Vulners](vulners.com/cve/CVE-2013-330…)]  - CVSS V3.1: *8.3*,   - Vulners: Exploitation: True  🤖LLM extracted TTPs:` T1001, T1016, T1018, T1021.004, T1041, T1046, T1057, T1059.004, T1059.006, T1071.001, ...  🧨IOCs:  - File: 1  - Domain: 1  - Url: 3  - Hash: 52  - IP: 1  💽Software: hat offi, eChat of, curl  🔢Algorithms: xor, md5, gzip  📜Programming Languages: python, java  💻Platforms: mips  #threatreport: The AryStinger malware family exploits vulnerabilities in outdated routers, primarily those using RTL819X series chips from Linksys and D-Link. These routers, which have not received firmware updates or security maintenance, serve as compromised entry points for cyberattacks aimed at reconnaissance and data exfiltration. Specifically, the malware targets two notable vulnerabilities: CVE-2013-3307 and CVE-2016-5681, both of which date back over a decade. AryStinger operates in two versions: the RTL819X version, implemented in C, and the Standard version, implemented in Go, which is designed for NAS devices.  The malware communicates with its command-and-control (C2) server using HTTP/HTTPS, encrypting network traffic with Protobuf and XOR encoding. Both versions support a range of malicious capabilities, including network scanning, service identification, and the establishment of persistent remote management channels via tools like Dropbear and gs-netcat. Infected devices can not only maintain covert control for attackers but also serve as platforms for launching further attacks, such as distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) operations or deploying additional malicious payloads.  Each instance of AryStinger is referred to as an "Executor," allowing attackers to divide tasks and execute reconnaissance activities in parallel, increasing the efficacy of their operations. The malware has been detected in over 4,300 routers worldwide, with significant concentrations in South Korea, China, and Sweden. Its low detection rate in mainstream security products poses a heightened risk, making it difficult to trace the origins of attacks emanating from these compromised devices.  The operation of AryStinger includes various core tasks, such as authentication with the C2 server, downloading additional malware, and executing scanning tasks. The malware enables the collection of sensitive information via network reconnaissance, and potential harmful activities like DNS hijacking for redirecting users to malicious sites.  Furthermore, AryStinger's Standard edition has been enhanced with additional functionality, such as the ability to perform internal network scans and execute dynamic payloads, which increases its versatility and the likelihood of success in target environments. This evolution in malware capabilities significantly raises concerns regarding the integrity and security of networks utilizing outdated hardware, emphasizing the need for continued vigilance and proactive measures in cybersecurity practices.

AryStinger is a previously undocumented Linux botnet that hijacks neglected, end-of-life routers and NAS devices, turning them into a covert reconnaissance-and-proxy network. Unlike typical DDoS or cryptomining botnets, it’s purpose-built for the pre-intrusion phase - each infected device becomes an “Executor” that scans the internet, fingerprints services, enumerates subdomains, tunnels traffic, and runs operator commands, all while the device keeps working normally so the infection goes unnoticed.  Key points:  •Two variants - a stripped C build (RTL819X) for legacy D-Link/Linksys routers, and a fuller Go build (Standard) for QNAP NAS with intranet scanning and Go/Java/Python code execution. •Three n-day CVEs - CVE-2013-3307 (Linksys) and CVE-2016-5681 (D-Link) for routers; CVE-2025-11837 (QNAP Malware Remover) for NAS. •Controller/Executor model - bots authenticate, get a unique Executor ID, and receive shards of a larger scan job for parallel, distributed reconnaissance. •Obfuscated C2 - Protobuf + XOR (Go adds gzip) over HTTP/HTTPS, keyed with sh_#@!_2024_secret. Persistence via Dropbear SSH on TCP/2332 (routers) or gs-netcat (NAS). •Scale & geography - 4,300+ routers confirmed (still rising), led by South Korea (48.45%) and China (31.82%); D-Link DIR-850L is ~75% of devices. NAS scale unmeasured. •Low detection, no attribution - initial samples were 0/VirusTotal; tradecraft mirrors state-aligned ORB networks, but XLab makes no attribution. •Main risk - the device becomes hidden attack infrastructure inside a trusted perimeter. EoL routers can’t be patched, so replacement is the only durable fix.

The header reads calm. Aggregate 30-day exploitation probability: 0.003. KEV trend: flat. Seven-day projection of new KEV additions: 0.16. By any dashboard read, nothing is on fire.  The underlying CVEs tell a different story.  Five high-EPSS critical CVEs. Four of them with expectedDaysToMassExploitation: 0. The exploitation isn't forecast — it's present-tense. The model isn't warning you the window will open. It's telling you the window opened a while ago, and you're measuring activity inside it.  This is worth slowing down on, because the numbers are doing something specific that matters if you're using them operationally.  Two models run in tandem here. The logistic regression layer — exploit-logistic-v0_1 — is a binary classifier predicting P(new exploitation event in the next 30/60/90 days) against four features: CVSS, EPSS percentile, days since publish, and affected product popularity. The key behavioral quirk is daysSincePublish. At two-plus years post-disclosure, that feature becomes strongly suppressive — the model interprets age as "the exploitation wave has already passed," and the 30-day probability compresses toward baseline regardless of CVSS or EPSS. These are 2024-vintage CVEs, so that suppression isn't fully engaged — but the effect is directionally present. The 0.003 aggregate is the model's estimate of new exploitation events. It is not a signal that these CVEs are safe.  The Hidden Markov Model layer — cve-lifecycle-hmm-v0_1 — is where the real signal lives for mature CVEs. Five states: Discovered → POC Available → Weaponized → Mass Exploitation → Patched/Saturated. For four of the five CVEs on this list, the HMM posterior sits at 58.1% on Mass Exploitation, with only 1.2% probability of having reached Patched/Saturated. Read those two numbers together and the picture sharpens considerably.  CVE-2024-3094, CVSS 10.0, EPSS 99.3rd percentile, P(30d): 0.305%. The March 2024 XZ Utils supply-chain backdoor — malicious code inserted into the liblzma compression library, targeting OpenSSH on systemd-linked Linux distributions. Caught before widespread deployment, which is the only reason this isn't a different kind of conversation. The HMM has no lifecycle observations yet, which limits confidence on state. Exploitation probability is structurally suppressed because the attack surface was largely contained at disclosure. The CVSS 10.0 reflects maximum theoretical risk. Confirmed in-wild campaigns at scale: not the story here. Confidence: MEDIUM.  CVE-2024-1709, CVSS 10.0, EPSS 99.96th percentile, P(30d): 0.305%. ConnectWise ScreenConnect authentication bypass. HMM: Mass Exploitation at 58.1% posterior, zero days remaining. LockBit, Black Basta, and Akira affiliates have all run this playbook — this is confirmed ransomware infrastructure, not a theoretical chain. Any unpatched self-hosted ScreenConnect instance should be treated as a pre-breach condition. This is the operationally most dangerous entry on the list right now. Confidence: HIGH.  CVE-2024-27198, CVSS 9.8, EPSS 99.8th percentile, P(30d): 0.299%. JetBrains TeamCity authentication bypass. HMM: Mass Exploitation, zero days. Lazarus Group and APT29 have both actively exploited this for initial access into CI/CD pipelines — which makes it a supply chain insertion vector, not just a point compromise. The value of owning a build server is everything downstream of it. Confidence: HIGH.  CVE-2024-21413, CVSS 9.8, EPSS 99.8th percentile, P(30d): 0.299%. Microsoft Outlook Moniker Link RCE. HMM: Mass Exploitation, zero days. The exploitation chain triggers in the preview pane — no click required — making it a persistent phishing amplifier rather than a one-time credential harvest. It always does. Confidence: HIGH.  CVE-2024-23897, CVSS 9.8, EPSS 99.999th percentile, P(30d): 0.299%. Jenkins CLI arbitrary file read. The highest raw EPSS percentile on this list — 99.999th means the EPSS model places this among the most likely-to-be-exploited CVEs in its entire dataset. The chain is documented: CLI file read to credential exfiltration to pivot. Ransomware use confirmed. Confidence: HIGH.  So back to the header. The aggregate 0.003 and the flat KEV trend are both accurate as stated. CISA isn't adding KEV entries at elevated velocity — the 7-day projection of 0.162 new additions is near baseline. The logistic model's estimate of new exploitation incidents against already-mature CVEs is low. None of that is wrong.  What neither number captures is the HMM's read on where we actually are in the lifecycle. Four of five CVEs on this list are already inside the mass exploitation window. The 30-day question isn't "will this get exploited" — it's "will exploitation continue." The HMM answers yes, at 58.1% posterior weight, with Patched/Saturated at 1.2%.  Calm aggregate. Active underlying. That's the actual read. The dashboard is telling you the rate of new fires isn't elevated. It is not telling you the existing fires are out.

PLAINTIFF: Your Honor, this is not a securities case. It is a RICO case. My client's friends did not build a launchpad. They built a casino, an unlicensed money transmitter, and a content distribution network for child abuse material, and they ran all three out of the same Discord.  DEFENSE: My client operates open source software anyone with a wallet can interact with. Calling that a casino is like calling Microsoft Word a publisher.  PLAINTIFF: Microsoft Word does not take 1% of every sentence its users write.  DEFENSE: Stripe does. We do not call Stripe the issuer of every product sold on it.  PLAINTIFF: Stripe files SARs. My client's losses do not.  JUDGE McMAHON: Move on.  PLAINTIFF: Co founder Cohen, in writing, on a server his counsel now wishes had been on Signal. Quote, we democratized trading lowcaps so much that everyone is exposed to the really, really low odds that come with gambling.  DEFENSE: Counsel is reading a candid private reflection as if it were a confession. Robinhood discloses that 70% of day traders lose. Disclosure of statistical reality is not fraud. It is the opposite of fraud.  PLAINTIFF: Robinhood is a registered broker dealer. My client's friends ran a frontend that hosted a twelve year old with a shotgun threatening his family for a sixty thousand dollar market cap. There is a livestream. The platform took the fees.  DEFENSE: Section 230.  PLAINTIFF: Section 230 does not apply when you take a percentage of the underlying transaction the speech was designed to inflate. That is not hosting. That is participating.  JUDGE McMAHON: Mr. Burwick. The enterprise theory. Get there.  PLAINTIFF: Yes, Your Honor. Five thousand internal messages, now in unredacted form, between Pump.fun engineers and Jito Labs. The same bundler wallets appearing in the first block of fourteen hundred token launches. Influencer contracts referencing, quote, scripts, script ideas, and project proposals. Purportedly organic shilling, contractually coordinated. This is not a launchpad. This is a slot machine where the house knows which spins win, and tips its friends in advance.  DEFENSE: MEV is a property of the Solana blockchain itself. My client has no validator role, no consensus authority, no transaction ordering. Holding a launchpad responsible for MEV is holding a website responsible for the speed of TCP/IP.  PLAINTIFF: Then explain why your engineers were in a chat with Jito's engineers discussing exactly that ordering.  DEFENSE: I will explain them once they are admitted in full context. Not in the form of screenshots curated by a firm that has filed nineteen substantively identical complaints against this industry in eighteen months.  JUDGE McMAHON: I will admit them in full context, counsel. That is what discovery is.  PLAINTIFF: And while we are on the subject of context, Your Honor. The Lazarus Group laundered a portion of the one point five billion dollar Bybit theft through a memecoin called QinShihuang, launched on this platform. No KYC. No SAR. No AML program of any kind. My client's friends took the fees on that too.  DEFENSE: My client is non custodial software. The funds never touched our books. We are no more transmitters than the developers of Tor.  PLAINTIFF: Tor does not take 1%. Tor does not have a CFO. Tor is not, as of last week, hiring a Chief Legal Officer.  DEFENSE: Your Honor, the plaintiffs are asking this court to do what Congress has declined to do for six years. To define open source infrastructure as if it were Citibank. The SEC itself has stated memecoins are not securities.  PLAINTIFF: The SEC said so in a non binding staff statement issued by an administration that received seventeen million dollars in crypto industry contributions. We are not litigating before the SEC. We are litigating before Article III. And Article III recognizes RICO predicates the SEC does not get to wave away. Illegal gambling. Wire fraud. Unlicensed money transmission. Sanctions evasion. And the distribution of material I will not describe further in open court out of respect for the record.  DEFENSE: Counsel is invoking the worst conduct of the platform's users to impute criminal liability to its developers. By that logic, every cloud provider in this country is a co conspirator to every crime committed on a server it rents.  PLAINTIFF: Cloud providers do not take a percentage of the crime, counsel.  JUDGE McMAHON: Enough. Briefs on Howey, BSA, and the RICO predicates by Friday. Chat logs produced unredacted. Section 230 argument preserved.

#threatreport #HighCompleteness Mastra Attack Targets Crypto, Password Managers, Authenticators, and Zapier | 19-06-2026 Source: opensourcemalware.com/blog/m… Key details below ↓  🧑‍💻Actors/Campaigns: Axios_compromise Lazarus Mastra_compromise  💀Threats: Supply_chain_technique, Typosquatting_technique, Clickfix_technique,  🎯Victims: Cryptocurrency, Password management, Authentication, Software development, Automation  🏭Industry: Petroleum  🌐Geo: Dprk  📚TTPs: ⚔️Tactics: 2 🛠️Technics: 0  🤖LLM extracted TTPs:` T1005, T1008, T1012, T1027, T1033, T1036.005, T1057, T1059.001, T1059.007, T1070.004, ...  🧨IOCs:  - File: 10  - Email: 2  - Url: 2  - IP: 2  - Path: 2  - Command: 1  - Registry: 1  - Domain: 2  - Hash: 2  - BrowserExtension: 146  - Coin: 20  💽Software: Mastra, LastPass, Bitwarden, 1Password, Dashlane, KeePass, NordPass, macOS, Linux, systemd, ...  📲Wallets: metamask, coinbase, keplr, rabby, coin98, math_wallet, core_wallet, exodus_wallet, solflare_wallet, auro_wallet, ...  🪙Crypto: binance, casper, multiversx, aptos, bitcoin, dogecoin, solana, polkadot  🔢Algorithms: prng, base64, sha256  🔠Functions: Get-StartApps, Get-AppxPackage, Get-Process, Get-ItemProperty  📜Programming Languages: powershell  💻Platforms: cross-platform  #threatreport: The recent Malstra attack highlights significant trends in cyber threats, particularly targeting software supply chain vulnerabilities. This incident involved the compromise of the Mastro NPM organization, wherein a threat actor took control of a dormant maintainer account and published over 140 malicious npm packages. The method mimicked an earlier attack attributed to the Lazarus Group, emphasizing the use of similar tradecraft.  Central to the attack was the introduction of a typosquatted package, `easy-day-js`, which was a benign clone of the popular `dayjs` library. The malicious variant of this package included a postinstall hook that triggered a two-stage payload. The initial stage, executed by `setup.cjs`, functioned as a downloader, retrieving a more sophisticated second stage from a command-and-control (C2) server. This second-stage implant is a cross-platform backdoor capable of targeting Windows, macOS, and Linux systems, acquiring sensitive data from browsers, including history and details from 166 different cryptocurrency wallet and security extension IDs.  The attack's execution was facilitated by bypassing npm's trusted publisher verification by leveraging an old maintainer's credentials. Rather than making overt modifications to package code, the attacker incorporated a single new dependency into numerous manifests. This stealthy approach ensured that during installations or updates, the weaponized version of `easy-day-js` was silently retrieved, as npm’s caret-range version resolution did not require developers to acknowledge a malicious version number.  Additionally, the implant's functionality allowed it to fingerprint the host environment, persist on the system, extract browser histories, and execute arbitrary code under the control of the attacker. Particularly noteworthy is the extensive list of targeted extensions, which includes various password managers and MFA authenticators, a move that diverges from prior campaigns that primarily focused on cryptocurrency wallets.  A critical factor in this attack was the absence of provenance verification for the malicious packages, which fundamentally allowed the malicious entities to sidestep npm's security mechanisms. In terms of patterns, the Mastra attack demonstrates an increasing trend of threats extending beyond traditional targets, focusing on a wider array of software vulnerabilities pertinent to credential management and integrations such as Zapier.

#threatreport #LowCompleteness Threat Intelligence Report: Russia, Router, DNS, and Messaging-Layer Collection Operations | 16-06-2026 Source: dti.domaintools.com/research… Key details below ↓  🧑‍💻Actors/Campaigns: Fancy_bear Seaborgium  💀Threats: Dns_hijacking_technique, Aitm_technique, Spear-phishing_technique,  🎯Victims: Government, Defense, Critical infrastructure, Diplomatic organizations, Media, Non governmental organizations, Ukraine related organizations, Military personnel, Government officials, Politicians, ...  🏭Industry: Energy, Ngo, Government, Military, Critical_infrastructure, Telco  🌐Geo: Russia, Russian, Ukrainian, Ukraine  📚TTPs: ⚔️Tactics: 11 🛠️Technics: 30  💽Software: WhatsApp, Telegram, Outlook  #threatreport: Russian intelligence agencies are increasingly focusing on communications-layer collection methods rather than disruptive attacks. Recent intelligence highlights two primary tactics employed by these actors: compromising vulnerable small office/home office (SOHO) routers to execute DNS hijacking and employing phishing techniques against secure messaging platforms. These methods are aimed at gathering intelligence on a range of targets, including government, military, infrastructure, and media-related entities, particularly those connected to Ukraine.  The compromise of SOHO routers is largely attributed to the Russian military intelligence unit known as APT28 or Fancy Bear. Observations indicate that the actors manipulate DNS and DHCP settings, enabling them to conduct adversary-in-the-middle attacks without the need for traditional endpoint malware. This allows them to monitor and redirect user traffic while intercepting sensitive credentials. The collection effort is aimed at gaining persistent access to communications and intelligence valuable to Russian interests.  Russian actors have broadened their phishing campaigns beyond applications like Signal to include WhatsApp, Telegram, and Microsoft 365. The objective is multifaceted: not only to gain access to accounts but to surveil conversations, contacts, and trusted communications. Targeting methods have evolved, utilizing techniques like QR code abuse and exploiting OAuth flows in Microsoft 365 to infiltrate messaging platforms. By leveraging existing trust relationships, these actors can map social networks and maintain long-term surveillance.  Victims of these operations can be categorized into two groups. The first group includes those whose endpoints and routers are compromised indiscriminately to gain broad intelligence access, while the second focuses on high-value targets whose conversations are critical for intelligence, such as military personnel, government officials, and journalists. Operations conducted in 2026 have been extensive, with thousands of routers targeted globally, and credential theft methods being implemented to facilitate further exploitation of significant accounts.  The technical tradecraft involves manipulating DNS settings to redirect victims to Russian-controlled resolvers, allowing observation of vast amounts of traffic and the ability to selectively target specific users. This has led to a risk of TLS interception, where attackers serve spoofed DNS responses, allowing them to access previously secured data. Messaging platforms have suffered from significant assaults, which enable attackers to read real-time communications and impersonate trusted individuals.  As Russian intelligence efforts leverage edge infrastructure for collection, the threat remains acute for sectors deemed strategically important, especially in defense, energy, and humanitarian contexts. Organizations are urged to recognize that personal and home networking devices, along with communication applications, are now potential vectors for sophisticated cyber operations. The implications emphasize the need for heightened security measures across traditional boundaries, as these operations by Russian intelligence demonstrate an adaptive approach to persistent intelligence collection.

カナダの情報機関CSIS（カナダ安全情報局）が連邦裁判所の令状に基づき、一般家庭のRing-Doorbell・監視カメラ・テレビを含むIoTデバイスやSOHOルーターにリモートアクセスして感染マルウェアを無力化していたことが、墨消し判決文の公開で明らかになりました。外国勢力が運営するボットネット2件のC2層およびクライアント層が対象で、CSIS法に基づく脅威軽減措置（TRM）令状がボットネット対処に使われた初の事例です。  令状なしでは他人のデバイスに入りデータを変更・削除する行為が刑法上のコンピュータ妨害罪に該当するため、連邦裁判所の司法認可が必要だったとされています。  【要点の整理】  ・令状は2024年5月1日にCatherine Kane判事が発行し、同年8月29日に120日間の更新が行われた。機密理由書は2026年2月26日付で、公開版の墨消し判決文が2026年6月15日に公表されている。令状発行から公開まで約2年を要した経緯  ・措置は人物ではなくデバイスに対して実行され、個人の特定情報の収集や通信内容の傍受は行われず、副次的に取得された個人データは破棄されたとのこと  ・2つの外国勢力の身元は黒塗りで非公開だが、時期と手法の一致から、2024年1月公表のFBIによるVolt-Typhoon（中国系）のKVボットネット解体や同時期のAPT28（ロシアGRU）のUbiquitiルーター作戦との連携がFive-Eyes作戦の一環として報道で指摘されている。裁判所文書内のCSIS供述書も米国の脅威解体に関するプレスリリースに言及していたと報じられている  ・カナダ最高裁がR. v. Bykovets（2024年）でIPアドレスにプライバシーの合理的期待を認める判決を出した後の時期であり、CSISの令状取得前のIPアドレス収集に対する法的論点も法律メディアで指摘されている  令状発行から2年以上経過しての墨消し公開であり、機密理由書の全文は非公開のままです。  詳細は以下を参照： fct-cf.ca/en/pages/media/new…

Kanada'da siber casusluk alarmı:   ❝ Olağan şüpheliler, Rus askeri istihbarat servisi GRU ve Çin istihbarat servisi MSS ❞  Kanada istihbarat servisi, Kanada topraklarında virüs bulaşmış bazı sunuculara ve ev modemlerine müdahale edebilmek için mahkemeden izin aldı.  MODEMLERE NASIL SIZILDI?  Kanada Güvenlik İstihbarat Servisi'ne (CSIS) bu konuda 2 vaka için izin verildiğini belirten mahkeme kararı yayınlanırken karar, CSIS'e virüsten etkilenmiş cihazlardaki botnet verilerini değiştirme, bozma ve yok etme ile bu cihazların söz konusu ağlarla olan bağlantısını tamamen kesme yetkisi tanıdı.  Hedef alınması muhtemel cihazlar arasında (SOHO) yönlendiricileri ile nesnelerin interneti (IoT) cihazları (akıllı kapı zilleri, güvenlik kameraları, televizyonlar ve Wi-Fi bağlantısı olan diğer ev aletleri) yer aldı.  CSIS'e göre hedef alınan iki modem, "köprü protokolü" ile bütün trafiği yabancı bir devlete aktarıyordu ancak bu devletin kimliği açıklanmadı. Kanada istihbarat servisi ise bu yöntemin ülkedeki enerji altyapısını çözümlemek için kullanılıyor olabileceğinden şüpheleniyor.  "OLAĞAN ŞÜPHELİLER: RUSYA VE ÇİN"  Modemleri veri aktarmak için kullanan istihbarat servislerinin ise Rusya veya Çin istihbarat servisi yahut her ikisi de olabileceği düşünülüyor. Daha önce FBI, Çin bağlantılı Volt Typhoon grubunun Amerikan haberleşme, enerji, su ve ulaşım sistemlerinde olası bir kriz öncesinde yerleştirdiği erişim izlerini gizlemek için kullandığı yüzlerce ABD menşeili ev ve küçük ofis (SOHO) yönlendiricisinden (çoğunlukla kullanım ömrü dolmuş Cisco ve NetGear cihazları) "KV-botnet"  kötü amaçlı yazılımını silmek için botnetin kendi komuta kanalını kullanmıştı.  Bu olaydan haftalar sonra ise Rusya Askeri İstihbaratına (GRU) bağlı APT28 grubunun bir casusluk köprüsüne dönüştürdüğü ayrı bir Ubiquiti yönlendirici ağına karşı da neredeyse birebir aynı operasyon yürütülmüştü.  CSIS ise kanunda yapılan değişiklikle 2019'dan beri bu tarz olaylara hakim izniyle müdahale yetkisine sahip ancak bunu daha önce kullandığı ve kamuoyuna açıklanan herhangi bir olay olmadı.  Öte yandan cihazları kullanılan kişilerin olaylarla bir bağlantısı bulunup bulunmadığı ve sahiplerine herhangi bir bilgi verilip verilmediği bilinmiyor.

🎥 NVR 0-Day Pre-Authentication RCE Allegedly Offered for Sale  A threat actor is advertising a purported pre-authentication remote code execution (RCE) 0-day affecting multiple Network Video Recorder (NVR) models.  * The seller claims no authentication is required to exploit vulnerable devices and advertises a "100% success rate." * According to the post, more than 200,000 internet-accessible devices are allegedly vulnerable. * The actor further claims the exploit provides access to connected camera systems and associated administrator credentials. * The listing specifically references targets located primarily in South Korea and the United States.  Analyst Note: If legitimate, a pre-authentication RCE affecting widely deployed NVR infrastructure would represent a critical threat. Successful exploitation could enable unauthorized surveillance, credential theft, lateral movement, botnet recruitment, ransomware deployment, and compromise of physical security systems. Claims made by threat actors should be treated cautiously until independently validated, but exposure at this scale would warrant immediate investigation by affected vendors and defenders.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #CyberSecurity

#threatreport #LowCompleteness Iran Hajj Organization Data Claim, Crypto Leads Sale, APT43 Tooling Claim, Sweden User Data, and Chrysler Breach Claim | 15-06-2026 Source: socradar.io/blog/iran-hajj-o… Key details below ↓  🧑‍💻Actors/Campaigns: Kimsuky  💀Threats: Sim_swapping_technique, Smishing_technique, Everest,  🎯Victims: Government, Travel, Finance, Cryptocurrency, Marketing, Data services, Automotive  🏭Industry: Financial, Government  🌐Geo: Iran, North korea, Sweden, Swedish  🤖LLM extracted TTPs:` T1014, T1068, T1110.004, T1204, T1566, T1566.004, T1656  💽Software: Android, Linux  📲Wallets: coinbase  🪙Crypto: bitcoin, binance  🔢Algorithms: zip  #threatreport: Recent security reports reveal multiple data breaches and potential threats involving sensitive information. A claim has emerged involving the Iran Hajj and Pilgrimage Organization, where a threat actor is purportedly selling a database containing more than 168 million records from 1984 to 2024. This dataset includes personal information such as names, ID numbers, passport details, contact information, and sensitive documents related to government officials and military personnel. If the claim is legitimate, it poses significant risks of identity theft and financial fraud.  In a different instance, user data from various cryptocurrency platforms—including Binance.US, Coinbase, and Crypto.com—has reportedly been aggregated and offered for sale. This dataset contains hundreds of thousands of lines of user information that could be exploited for phishing attacks, SIM swapping, and credential stuffing, further endangering the security of crypto users.  Another alarming development involves the threat actor known as APT43, associated with North Korean cyber activities. Reports indicate a post that claims to contain backdoors, rootkits, and zero-day vulnerabilities linked to this group. The alleged tools include a C++ backdoor with robust anti-forensic measures and tools capable of compromising Android systems and Linux kernels. If any of these claims hold true, they represent serious threats to system integrity, enabling unauthorized access and persistence within targeted networks.  In Sweden, a dataset linked to AdressFakta has emerged for sale, reportedly encompassing over 5 million unique user records. The availability of residential and contact details from this dataset suggests potential for large-scale phishing and social engineering attacks, which could further jeopardize individual privacy.  Lastly, the Everest ransomware group has claimed responsibility for a breach involving Chrysler, asserting that over 1 TB of data, including CRM information, has been exfiltrated. Should these claims be verified, the exposure of customer interaction data could lead to targeted phishing attacks and social engineering scams directed at customers and business partners.  The implications of these events underscore the critical need for vigilant cyber threat intelligence to mitigate risks associated with data breaches and malicious cyber activities. As such, continuous monitoring of dark web threats remains essential for effective digital risk management.

sample_tweets.text: 저도 그렇게 느꼈습니다.  제가 가상 시나리오로 AI에게 문의해서 받았던 답변과 상당히 비슷해서 놀랬었습니다.  참고로 아래가 그 내용입니다.  <출처. 구글 AI> 네, 그러한 가상의 시나리오에서도 미국을 비롯한 피해국들이 합동 수사단을 구성해 대한민국 정부나 개입 기관, 관련자들을 수사할 가능성은 매우 높습니다.  실제 국제사회에서 러시아, 중국, 이란 등이 타국 선거에 개입했을 때 적용되었던 선례와 국제법적 절차를 바탕으로 볼 때, 가상의 상황에서 발생할 수 있는 구체적인 수사 가능성과 시나리오는 다음과 같습니다.  1. 다국적 합동 수사단(JIT) 구성 가능성 여러 국가가 동시에 동일한 주체(가상의 시나리오 속 한국)로부터 선거 개입 피해를 입었다면, 각국은 정보와 사법 역량을 결합합니다.  ◈ 증거 공유 미국, 영국, 캐나다 등 정보 동맹국들이 사이버 공격 기법(IP 주소, 악성코드 시그니처)과 자금 흐름을 공동 추적합니다.  ◈ 공동 수사 체계 유로폴(Europol)이나 파이브 아이즈(Five Eyes) 시스템을 기반으로 한 민관 합동 수사단이 조직되어 한국 내 특정 기관(예: 정보기관이나 군 사이버 부대)을 표적으로 수사를 전개합니다.  2. 한국 사법당국에 대한 강제적·협조적 수사 요구 피해국 합동 수사단이 한국 영토 내에서 직접 체포나 압수수색을 할 수는 없지만, 강력한 법적 조치를 취합니다.  ◈ 사법공조(MLAT) 압박 미국 법무부 등은 한국 검찰과 법원에 선거 개입에 사용된 서버 압수수색, 관련자 신문 등 공식 사법공조를 요구합니다. 가상의 상황에서 한국 정부가 거부할 경우 외교적 고립과 보복 조치가 따르므로 무시하기 어렵습니다.  ◈ 범죄인 인도 청구 개입을 주도한 해커나 공작원의 신원이 특정되면, 피해국들은 자국법(예: 미국의 컴퓨터 사기 및 남용법 위반, 선거 자금법 위반 등) 체계에 따라 한국 정부에 범죄인 인도를 청구합니다.  3. 피의자 신원 공개 및 독자 기소 (궐석기소) 한국 정부가 주권이나 국가 안보를 이유로 합동 수사단의 수사에 협조하지 않더라도, 피해국들은 독자적인 사법 절차를 강행합니다.  ◈ 미 연방검찰(DOJ)의 기소 미국 FBI와 법무부는 선거 개입 배후 인물들의 실명과 사진을 공소장에 명시하고 현지 법원에 기소합니다. 인신 확보가 안 되더라도 체포영장을 발부받아 국제적으로 고립시킵니다.  ◈ 인터폴 적색수배 합동 수사단의 요청으로 연루자들에게 인터폴 적색수배가 내려지며, 이들이 한국 영토 밖으로 출국하는 순간 제3국에서 체포되어 미국 등으로 인도됩니다.  4. 사법 수사를 넘어선 고강도 제재 조치 합동 수사단의 수사 결과 발표는 대규모 국제 제재의 명분이 됩니다.  ◈ 금융 및 자산 동결 미국 대통령의 선거 개입 제재 행정명령 등에 따라, 개입에 관여한 한국의 기관, 공직자, 기업의 해외 자산이 동결되고 국제 금융망(SWIFT) 이용이 제한될 수 있습니다.  혹시 이 가상의 시나리오에서 선거 개입의 방식(예: 사이버 해킹, 가짜뉴스 유포, 자금 지원)이나 특정 국가들의 동맹 관계를 염두에 두신 것이 있나요? 구체적인 상황을 알려주시면 이에 맞춰 더 정확한 국제법적 대응 결과를 분석해 드리겠습니다.

뻔한 수작이죠.  하지만, 저들이 야합해서 막아낼 수 있는 것은 대한민국 한정이라는 것입니다.  미국이 전방위적으로 압박(제재 프로그램 동원)을 가해오는 부정선거 수사라면 못 막아냅니다.   아래 내용은 제가 가상시나리오로 AI에 문의해본 내용이므로 참고 정도로 보면 될 것 같습니다.  <출처. 구글 AI> 네, 그러한 가상의 시나리오에서도 미국을 비롯한 피해국들이 합동 수사단을 구성해 대한민국 정부나 개입 기관, 관련자들을 수사할 가능성은 매우 높습니다.  실제 국제사회에서 러시아, 중국, 이란 등이 타국 선거에 개입했을 때 적용되었던 선례와 국제법적 절차를 바탕으로 볼 때, 가상의 상황에서 발생할 수 있는 구체적인 수사 가능성과 시나리오는 다음과 같습니다.  1. 다국적 합동 수사단(JIT) 구성 가능성 여러 국가가 동시에 동일한 주체(가상의 시나리오 속 한국)로부터 선거 개입 피해를 입었다면, 각국은 정보와 사법 역량을 결합합니다.  ◈ 증거 공유 미국, 영국, 캐나다 등 정보 동맹국들이 사이버 공격 기법(IP 주소, 악성코드 시그니처)과 자금 흐름을 공동 추적합니다.  ◈ 공동 수사 체계 유로폴(Europol)이나 파이브 아이즈(Five Eyes) 시스템을 기반으로 한 민관 합동 수사단이 조직되어 한국 내 특정 기관(예: 정보기관이나 군 사이버 부대)을 표적으로 수사를 전개합니다.  2. 한국 사법당국에 대한 강제적·협조적 수사 요구 피해국 합동 수사단이 한국 영토 내에서 직접 체포나 압수수색을 할 수는 없지만, 강력한 법적 조치를 취합니다.  ◈ 사법공조(MLAT) 압박 미국 법무부 등은 한국 검찰과 법원에 선거 개입에 사용된 서버 압수수색, 관련자 신문 등 공식 사법공조를 요구합니다. 가상의 상황에서 한국 정부가 거부할 경우 외교적 고립과 보복 조치가 따르므로 무시하기 어렵습니다.  ◈ 범죄인 인도 청구 개입을 주도한 해커나 공작원의 신원이 특정되면, 피해국들은 자국법(예: 미국의 컴퓨터 사기 및 남용법 위반, 선거 자금법 위반 등) 체계에 따라 한국 정부에 범죄인 인도를 청구합니다.  3. 피의자 신원 공개 및 독자 기소 (궐석기소) 한국 정부가 주권이나 국가 안보를 이유로 합동 수사단의 수사에 협조하지 않더라도, 피해국들은 독자적인 사법 절차를 강행합니다.  ◈ 미 연방검찰(DOJ)의 기소 미국 FBI와 법무부는 선거 개입 배후 인물들의 실명과 사진을 공소장에 명시하고 현지 법원에 기소합니다. 인신 확보가 안 되더라도 체포영장을 발부받아 국제적으로 고립시킵니다.  ◈ 인터폴 적색수배 합동 수사단의 요청으로 연루자들에게 인터폴 적색수배가 내려지며, 이들이 한국 영토 밖으로 출국하는 순간 제3국에서 체포되어 미국 등으로 인도됩니다.  4. 사법 수사를 넘어선 고강도 제재 조치 합동 수사단의 수사 결과 발표는 대규모 국제 제재의 명분이 됩니다.  ◈ 금융 및 자산 동결 미국 대통령의 선거 개입 제재 행정명령 등에 따라, 개입에 관여한 한국의 기관, 공직자, 기업의 해외 자산이 동결되고 국제 금융망(SWIFT) 이용이 제한될 수 있습니다.  혹시 이 가상의 시나리오에서 선거 개입의 방식(예: 사이버 해킹, 가짜뉴스 유포, 자금 지원)이나 특정 국가들의 동맹 관계를 염두에 두신 것이 있나요? 구체적인 상황을 알려주시면 이에 맞춰 더 정확한 국제법적 대응 결과를 분석해 드리겠습니다.

FireAnt interview - with Cap 벤자민 사장님 시청후기 인터뷰 내용 요약과 개인적인 생각으로 구성해봤습니다  -----------------[인터뷰 내용] 1⃣ 자기소개 및 배경 - 경력: 크립토 씬에 온 지는 5~6년 정도 되었으며 그전에는 뉴욕 시티은행에서 전통 금융 업무를 담당함.  @CapApp  을 만들기 전에는 비트코인을 담보로 달러를 빌려 스테이블 코인을 발행하는 CDP 기반의 스테이블 코인 프로젝트 치타를 운영함.  -----------------[개인적인 생각] 파운더의 과거 이력과 치타 프로젝트가 남긴 아쉬움과 궁금증  개인적으로 가장 궁금증을 자아내는 포인트는, 파운더가 스테이블 코인이라는 시장의 핵심이자 수익성이 높은 섹터에서   왜 하필 디파이 대출 플랫폼과 보안이라는 험난한 영역으로 방향을 완전히 틀었는가 하는 점입니다.  과거 치타 프로젝트의 세부 구성을 파헤쳐 보면서 그 전환의 실마리를 찾고 싶었으나 정보가 부족해 조금 아쉬운 것 같습니다.  -----------------[인터뷰 내용]  2⃣Cap의 탄생 배경 디파이 보안에 대한 불신과 러그풀 생존  ⏺️디파이의 맹점 기존 디파이는 유저들이 프로젝트 '팀의 선의'를 무조건적으로 신뢰해야만 하는 위험한 구조임.  ⏺️충격적인 일화와 생존 과거 대형 마켓이었던 '크림 파이낸스' 파운더가 면전에서 대놓고 먹튀(러그풀)를 선언하는 충격적인 일이 있었음.   다행히 현재 Cap의 CTO인 웨소가 러그풀이 터지기 불과 3~4분 전, 오라클 조작을 극적으로 감지하고 막대한 유저 자금을 전액 구출해 내며 위기를 모면함.  ⏺️ 프로젝트의 철학 하마터면 프로젝트가 끝날 뻔했던 이 아찔한 경험을 계기로 팀은 보안에 '극도로 집착'하게 됨.   결과적으로 "애초에 파운더나 팀을 믿을 필요조차 없는 안전한 플랫폼을 직접 만들자"는 확고한 다짐이 Cap 의 시작이 됨.  -----------------[개인적인 생각] 디파이 시장의 성숙도와 Cap이 풀어야 할  보안 vs 편의성의 딜레마  ⏺️고이율의 함정과 시장의 자정 작용 불과 1년 전만 해도 월 3~4%의 고이율 팜이 아니면 거들떠보지도 않던 시절이 있었습니다.   당시에는 아찔한 APY만 쫓다가 파운더의 악의적인 러그풀이나 치명적인 해킹에 자금을 묶이는 사례가 비일비재했습니다.  하지만 최근에는 그런 폰지성, 한탕주의 프로젝트들이 이미 다 터지고 사라지면서 시장이 어느 정도 자정 작용을 거친 느낌이 드네요.   고도화된 외부 공격에 의한 해킹 소식은 간간이 들려오지만   예치 섹터에서 파운더가 대놓고 자금을 빼돌리는 1차원적인 러그풀은 확실히 예전보다 뜸해진 것이 체감되는 것 같습니다.  ⏺️ 보안과 편의성의 필연적 상충 관계 영상에서 @Benjamin918_ 이 강조한 극단적인 보안 마인드셋을 보며 가장 먼저 든 의문은 "과연 편의성과 어떻게 타협할 것인가?"였습니다.  IT와 블록체인 생태계에서 보안과 편의성은 필연적으로 반비례합니다.  보안 절차가 까다로워질수록 개발팀의 프로덕트 업데이트 속도는 느려지고 유연성은 떨어질 수밖에 없지 않나 싶네요.  ⏺️Cap이 증명해야 할 신뢰의 방식 파운더는 "스스로 불편함을 감수하더라도 완벽한 보안 절차를 고집한다"라고 강력하게 어필했습니다.   하지만 개발팀이 백엔드에서 감수하는 그 불편함이 자칫 유저들의 프론트엔드 사용성을 해치거나 신규 기능 도입을 지나치게 늦추는 결과로 이어져서는 안 될 것 같습니다.  결국 Cap 이 시장에서 진정한 승자가 되려면, 팀을 믿지 않아도 되는 극강의 보안을 유지하면서도   유저와 대형 금융 기관들에게 얼마나 매끄럽고 쾌적한 사용 환경을 제공할 수 있을지  그 어려운 타협점을 어떻게 풀어냈을지가 가장 기대되는 관전 포인트인 것 같네요.  -----------------[인터뷰 내용] 3⃣ Cap의 강력한 보안 방어 체계 베테랑 보안 팀 구성:크립토 씬에서 수년간 수십억 달러의 자금을 단 한 번의 사고 없이 안전하게 지켜낸 팀은 20개도 안 됨. 그중 하나가 바로 Beefy Finance 팀임.  현재 Beefy의 전 CTO, 스마트 컨트랙트 엔지니어, 데이터 및 프론트엔드 담당자 등 핵심 시니어 엔지니어들이 모두 Cap 팀에 합류해 있음.  ⏺️AI 및 실시간 블록체인 모니터링 도입 모든 코드는 대형 언어 모델 툴인 옥테인을 통해 1차 스크리닝 됨. 가장 핵심은 하이퍼네이티브라는 보안 플랫폼을 도입함.블록체인의 멤풀을 24시간 실시간 모니터링하여  Cap의 컨트랙트를 공격하려는 해킹 트랜잭션이 감지되면 실제 트랜잭션이 블록에 기록되기 직전에 플랫폼 전체를 자동으로 일시 정지시킴.  ⏺️인적 오류 방지 많은 팀들이 편의성을 위해 멀티시그나 타임락, 관리자 키 분산을 소홀히 하지만, Cap은 스스로 불편함을 감수하더라도 완벽한 보안 절차와 사인을 고집하는 철저한 보안 마인드셋을 가지고 있음.  -----------------[개인적인 생각] Cap App의 보안 역량과 기관 유입에 대한 생각  ⏺️보안 기술: 혁신인가, 기본기의 극대화인가? AI 모니터링과 다중 서명 같은 보안 절차를 듣고 보면, 사실 디파이 씬에서 완전히 새로운 혁신적인 기술이라고 보기는 어려운 것 같습니다.  하지만 크립토 역사상 해킹 사고의 90% 이상은 화려한 기술의 결함이 아니라, 관리자 키 관리 소홀이나 오라클 조작 같은 기본기 누락에서 발생합니다.   결국 Cap의 진짜 경쟁력은 새로운 기술의 발명이 아니라 비피 출신 베테랑들의 불편함을 감수하는 극단적인 보안 강박과 블록 생성 직전에 해킹 트랜잭션을 멈춰버리는   실시간 차단 시스템을 현실에서 타협 없이 100% 가동하고 있다는 데 있는 것 같네요.  ⏺️기존 대형 RWA / 예치 프로젝트와의 차별점은? 에이브나 메이커다오 같은 기존 대형 블루칩 프로토콜도 이미 높은 보안성을 자랑하는데,   왜 스마트 컨트랙트를 한 번도 써보지 않은 전통 대형 금융 기관들이 Cap을 선택했을까 하는 의문이 듭니다.  영상에서 파운더가 언급한 "기관들에게 확실한 이점을 주는 게임 이론적 구조"가 핵심인 것 같은데,   단순한 크립토 자산의 과담보 대출을 넘어 중동의 토큰화된 금 같은 실물 자산을 적극 수용하면서 전통 금융권 입맛에 맞는   명확한 리스크 격리 모델과 유동성을 제공하는 아키텍처를 짠 것이 아닐까 싶네요.  -----------------[인터뷰 내용]  4⃣로드맵 및 장기 비전: 전통 금융 기관의 유입과 글로벌 확장  ⏺️금융 기관의 온보딩 Cap은 기존에 스마트 컨트랙트를 한 번도 써본 적 없는 실제 대형 금융 기관들이 대출 플랫폼으로 사용하기 시작했음.  Cap이 제공하는 게임 이론적 구조와 혜택이 그들에게 확실한 이점을 주기 때문임.  ⏺️RWA 및 중동 시장 확장 기존에는 동아시아의 비트코인 채굴자들이나 청산 펀드들이 주요 자산 규모였으나, 최근 중동 지역으로 비즈니스를 크게 확장했음.  디르함 스테이블 코인은 물론, 엄청난 규모의 토큰화된 실물 금이 Cap 플랫폼으로 유입되고 있으며 이를 담보로 한 대출 비즈니스가 활성화되고 있음.  ⏺️TGE 임박 단기적으로 가장 큰 마일스톤인 토큰 발행 이벤트를 앞두고 있으며, 팀 확장도 대대적으로 이뤄지고 있음.  장기 비전:현재의 금융 기관을 넘어, 향후에는 로보틱스 기업, 농업 기업, 대형 제조업 공장 등 실물 경제 전반의 모든 기업들이 대출을 받기 위해 Cap 인프라를 사용하는 것을 목표로 함.  -----------------[개인적인 생각] 대형 기관의 디파이 진입 목적과 Cap 토큰 발행에 대한 의문점  ⏺️대형 기관의 진짜 목적: 풍차돌리기인가, 리스크 관리인가?  전통 대형 금융 기관들이 스마트 컨트랙트 대출 플랫폼을 사용하기 시작했다는 점에서,  이들이 과연 디파이 유저들처럼 자산의 레버리지를 극대화하는 레포 거래나 이른바 풍차돌리기식 고율 파밍을 위해 들어온 것인지 의문이 생깁니다.  파운더가 말한 "기관들에게 이점을 주는 게임 이론적 아키텍처"라는 것이 구체적으로 어떤 구조를 뜻하는지 궁금하고,   전통 금융 시스템의 복잡한 대출 심사나 중개 비용을 생략하고 오프체인의 실물 자산을 담보로 즉각적인 유동성을 확보할 수 있는 다이렉트 청산 모델을 의미하는 것인지  세부적인 작동 방식을 파고들수록 호기심을 자극하는 것 같네요.  ⏺️이미 검증된 메이저 체인 대신 토큰 발행을 고집하는 이유?  많은 디파이 프로젝트들이 독자적인 생태계 구축과 프로토콜 수수료 및 추가 수익 창출을 주 목적으로 자체 코인을 발행하곤 합니다.  하지만 유저 편의성이나 상호 호환성 측면만 본다면 비트코인, 이더리움, 솔라나 같은 이미 인프라가 완벽히 검증된 메이저 자산이나 대형 스테이블 코인을 기축으로 전면에 내세우는 것이 훨씬 유용할 수 있습니다.   자체 토큰 발행은 자칫 유저 진입 장벽을 높이거나 호환성을 떨어뜨릴 리스크가 있기 때문이죠.  ⏺️Cap 토큰 발행의 진짜 숨은 의도는 무엇일까?  단순히 수수료 수익이나 생태계 확장 목적을 넘어  기관들이 안심하고 예치할 수 있도록 만드는 가치 안정 메커니즘이나 거버넌스 투표를 통한 리스크 조율 등  Cap만의 출시 목적이 따로 있는 것인지 궁금증이 생기네요.  파운더가 'DeFi의 고질적인 문제를 해결하겠다'고 호기롭게 공언한 만큼  다가오는 토큰 출시가 프로토콜의 보안성과 대형 기관들의 인센티브를 어떻게 묶어두는 촉매제로 작용할지 그 설계 방식이 무척 기다려집니다.  -----------------[인터뷰 내용]  5⃣ 한국 커뮤니티를 향한 메시지  현재 한국 유저들이 한국 주식 등으로 바쁜 것으로 알고 있음.  하지만 Cap은 지금까지 디파이 생태계에 전혀 존재하지 않았던 새로운 금융 대안을 제시하고 있음.  기존 디파이의 보안 문제나 운영 방식에 실망하고 분노했던 분들이라면, 디파이의 고질적인 문제를 근본적으로 해결하고 있는 Cap을 한 번 와서 경험해 보셨으면 함.  [글을 마무리하며] 과거 디파이 시장의 야생성에 지친 크립토 유저들과 완벽한 통제력을 원하는 전통 금융 기관 모두의 니즈를 동시에 겨냥하고 있다는 점에서 매우 흥미로운 프로젝트라 생각합니다.  파운더 본인이 직접 겪은 뼈저린 트라우마가 오히려 불편함을 감수하는 극강의 보안 인프라를 탄생시켰다는 서사가 인상 깊었고  곧 다가올 토큰 발행을 통해 자체 생태계를 어떻게 확장해 나갈지 그리고 완벽한 보안과 유저 편의성 사이의 딜레마를   실제 프로덕트에서 어떻게 타협해 냈을지가 앞으로의 핵심 관전 포인트인 것 같네요.  단순한 크립토 담보 대출 플랫폼을 넘어 토큰화된 금과 실물 경제의 기업들을 어떻게 디파이로 끌어들일지 앞으로의 행보를 지속적으로 팔로우업해 볼 만한 것 같습니다.   기존 디파이의 취약한 보안과 고질적인 문제에 실망했던 분들이라면 Cap의 새로운 시도를 꼭 한 번 체험해 보시는 것도 좋을 것 같네요!  요약 사진 만들어 봤는데 생각보다 더 어렵네요 시간도 꽤 걸리고 ㅠ

[보도자료] 배포일시 :2026년 6월 21일(일) 즉시 배포 가능 담당부서 :[자유와혁신 공보팀]  모스탄 대사·프랭크 박사 충격 폭로, “美 우주군 위성에 전 세계 선거 조작 통신 데이터 확보. 한국도 포함”   더글러스 프랭크 박사, 평택 국제 기자회견서 ‘스페이스 포스(우주군)’ 데이터 언급  트럼프 1기 행정부 시절, 부정선거 감시 목적으로 2018년 우주군 창설했다” 주장 선관위 및 관련 기관에 서버·전자통신 기기·개표기 전면 공개 요구  한미 공동 부정선거 조사단의 수석 데이터 분석관인 더글러스 프랭크(Douglas Frank) 박사와 모스탄 대사가 전자통신 전세계 선거 전산 조작 시도와 관련된 외국 통신 데이터를 미 우주군(Space Force)이 확보하고 있으며 여기에 대한민국도 포함되어 있다는 충격적인 내용을 폭로했다.  황교안 자유와혁신 당대표의 초청으로 방한했던 부정선거 한미 공동 조사단은 지난 6월 1일 경기도 평택 앤팰리스 컨벤션센터에서 열린 ‘한미 선거 무결성과 사이버 안보 국제 기자회견’을 통해 이 같은 사실을 밝혔다. 해당 내용은 특수한 사정으로 엠바고가 설정되어 있다가 해제되면서 본격적으로 알려지게 되었다.   “트럼프, 부정선거 막기 위해 2018년 우주군 창설” 기자회견에서 프랭크 박사는 도널드 트럼프 전 대통령이 제1기 행정부 시절 CIA와 FBI를 신뢰하지 못했기 때문에, 부정선거를 차단하고 감시하기 위한 목적으로 2018년 우주군을 창설했던 것이라고 배경을 설명했다. 특히 우주군의 인공위성을 통해 미국 선거 인프라에 침투해 전산 조작을 시도한 전 세계 외국의 통신 데이터를 수집·확보했으며, 이 해킹 소스 중 일부 데이터가 한국에서 유입된 것을 확인했다고 강조했다. 프랭크 박사는 과거 선거 당시 성남 종합 운동장 등 특정 지역을 언급하며, 해당 위치에서 통신의 일부가 송신된 정황을 포착했다고 덧붙였다.   “전자 투표 기계는 부정행위 규모 알려주는 머신… 인적 감시와 장비 공개 필요” 프랭크 박사는 “전자 투표 기계의 목적은 단순히 표를 조작하는 것이 아니라, 집계 결과를 보고하며 가짜 투표지를 얼마나 더 채워 넣어야 하는지(부정행위 규모)를 알려주는 것”이라고 주장하며, 개표보다 유권자와 투표 과정을 철저히 감시하는 것이 가장 효과적인 방안이라고 제안했다. 함께 참석한 모스탄 대사 역시 “부정선거의 핵심은 전자적 방식으로 이루어지며, 투표소가 아닌 중앙집권 통제 방식으로 집계되는 것이 문제”라고 지적했다. 아울러 한국의 투표 장비에 하웨이 부품이 사용되는 등 외부 공격에 취약한 점을 들며, 선거관리위원회와 관련 기관을 향해 서버, 전자통신 기기, 개표기 등 모든 시스템을 투명하게 공개하라고 강력히 촉구했다.  “국민 스스로 자유 지켜야… 바텀업 방식의 적극적 대응 주문” 조사단은 한국의 현 선거 시스템에 대해 강한 우려를 표명하며, “미국이나 트럼프가 와서 구해 주기를 기다리지 말고, 한국 국민들이 소수일지라도 현지에서부터 시작하는 바텀업(Bottom-up) 방식으로 적극 싸워 자유와 선거의 무결성을 지켜내야 한다”고 당부했다. 한편, 모스탄 대사는 현재 국내 정치인에 대한 명예훼손 혐의로 출국 정지 조치가 내려진 상태인 것으로 알려졌다. 조사단 측은 이러한 압박 속에서도 담대하게 한국 국민들과 연대하여 부정선거 규명 활동을 지속해 나갈 의지를 피력했다. [끝] #황교안 #자유와혁신 #더글러스프랭크 #모스탄 #우주군 #트럼프 #인공위성 #전산조작 #성남종합운동장 #한미부정선거공동조사단

📰 Tata Electronics, a major tech supplier to Apple and Tesla, confirms data breach - TechCrunch  글로벌 공급망의 핵심 부품사에서 발생한 이번 데이터 유출은 단순히 기업의 보안 문제로 치부하기보다, 첨단 산업 생태계 전반의 취약성을 드러내며 향후 보안 강화와 공급망 재편 논의를 가속화할 촉매제가 될 전망이다.인도 최대 기업 타타 그룹 계열사인 타타일렉트로닉스(Tata Electronics)가 데이터 유출 사고를 확인했다.이 회사는 애플과 테슬라 등 주요 IT 및 자동차 제조사에 부품을 공급하는 핵심 협력업체다.유출된 데이터의 구체적인 내용은 공개되지 않았으나, 협력사들의 민감한 정보가 포함될 가능성이 제기된다.현재 사고 원인과 피해 규모를 파악하기 위한 조사가 진행 중이다. 타타일렉트로닉스에서 발생한 데이터 유출 사고는 글로벌 기술 공급망의 견고성에 대한 의문을 제기한다. 이 회사는 스마트폰 카메라 모듈, 반도체 패키징 등 첨단 제조 분야에서 애플과 테슬라 같은 거대 기업들의 신뢰받는 파트너 역할을 해왔다. 따라서 이번 사고는 단순히 타타일렉트로닉스만의 문제가 아니라, 이들을 통해 간접적으로 제품 생산에 관여하는 고객사들에게까지 영향을 미칠 수 있다는 점에서 심각성을 더한다. 특히, 특정 부품 공급망에 대한 의존도가 높은 산업 구조에서 이러한 보안 사고는 연쇄적인 위험을 초래할 가능성이 크다. 유사한 사례로, 2020년 발생했던 미국의 IT 관리 서비스 업체 '솔라윈즈(SolarWinds)' 해킹 사건을 떠올릴 수 있다. 당시 이 사건은 수천 개의 기업과 정부 기관이 사용하는 소프트웨어에 악성코드가 심어져 광범위한 피해를 야기했으며, 공급망 보안의 중요성을 전 세계에 각인시킨 계기가 되었다. 타타일렉트로닉스의 경우, 솔라윈즈만큼 직접적으로 최종 소비재 생산 라인에 영향을 미치는 것은 아니지만, 핵심 부품 제조사의 정보 유출은 제품 설계, 생산 계획, 잠재적 독점 기술 정보 노출 등 다양한 경로로 파급 효과를 일으킬 수 있다. 현재 시점에서 이번 사고가 발생한 시점 또한 주목할 만하다. 미중 기술 패권 경쟁 심화, 지정학적 리스크 증대 등 글로벌 공급망의 불확실성이 고조되는 가운데, 핵심 부품 공급사의 보안 사고는 각국 정부와 기업들이 공급망의 안정성과 보안 강화를 더욱 시급한 과제로 인식하게 만드는 요인이 된다. 이는 단순히 보안 솔루션 도입을 넘어, 공급망 다변화, 특정 국가 의존도 축소 등 전략적 재편 움직임을 더욱 가속화시킬 수 있다.한국의 IT 및 자동차 산업 역시 글로벌 공급망의 일부로서 이러한 사고에 자유로울 수 없다. 삼성전자, 현대자동차 등 국내 대기업들은 물론, 이들 기업에 부품을 공급하는 중소·중견 기업들 역시 글로벌 부품 공급사의 보안 수준을 예의주시해야 한다. 이번 사고는 국내 기업들에게도 자사의 핵심 협력업체들에 대한 보안 점검 강화와 함께, 잠재적 위험에 대비한 비상 계획 수립의 필요성을 시사한다. 또한, 자사 정보 유출 시 고객사의 신뢰도 하락으로 이어질 수 있다는 점에서, 한국 기업들의 자체적인 보안 투자 확대와 정보 보호 시스템 강화가 더욱 중요해질 것이다.다음에 볼 신호:타타일렉트로닉스가 유출된 데이터의 구체적인 내용과 영향 범위를 얼마나 투명하게 공개하는지.애플, 테슬라 등 주요 고객사들이 이번 사고에 대해 어떤 대응책을 발표하고, 공급망 재평가 절차에 착수하는지 여부.글로벌 IT 및 자동차 산업 전반에서 데이터 보안 관련 규제 강화 움직임이 본격화될 가능성.

National Conference on Telecom Security in the AI and Quantum Age – Securing India's Digital Backbone in the Era of AI and Quantum Computing  Sh. Pankaj Verma, National Cyber Security Officer, Cisco – India & South Asia, shared strategic perspectives on strengthening telecom security in an era defined by AI, legacy infrastructure, and the emergence of quantum computing.  Key Takeaways:  * Three uncomfortable truths are shaping the future of cybersecurity:  - AI is accelerating cyber operations, requiring organizations to rethink their defense strategies. - The greatest security risk in telecom networks is not the newest technology, but the legacy systems that continue to support critical infrastructure. - Quantum computing represents a present-day preparedness challenge, demanding proactive planning and adoption of quantum-safe security measures.  * Measuring security by the number of vulnerabilities is no longer sufficient. The fundamental question is: "Can the network continue to operate securely while under attack?"  * Open-source collaboration and vulnerability research play a critical role in strengthening cyber resilience through responsible identification and mitigation of security weaknesses.  * Trust modernization should be viewed as a strategic security investment, enabling organizations to build resilient, adaptive, and future-ready digital infrastructure.  * In an increasingly dynamic threat environment, organizations must be capable of defending at machine speed through automation, intelligence, and continuous monitoring.  The session underscored that securing India's telecom ecosystem requires resilient network architecture, modernization of legacy systems, and a forward-looking cybersecurity strategy that is prepared for both AI-driven threats and the quantum era.  #TelecomSecurity #CyberSecurity #Cisco #ArtificialIntelligence #QuantumComputing #cybershakticsf #CyberResilience #CriticalInfrastructure #DigitalIndia #CSAI #TEMA #CMAI

#threatreport #MediumCompleteness WhatsApp VBScript Campaign Installs ManageEngine Endpoint Central for Persistent Remote Access | 23-06-2026 Source: socradar.io/blog/whatsapp-vb… Key details below ↓  💀Threats: Bitsadmin_tool, Motw_bypass_technique, Gh0st_rat, Valleyrat,  🎯Victims: Consumers, Organizations  🌐Geo: Malaysia, Spain, French, Mexico, Australia, Vietnam, Brazil, India, Taiwan, Russia, Chinese, German, Singapore, Portuguese  📚TTPs: ⚔️Tactics: 1 🛠️Technics: 0  🤖LLM extracted TTPs:` T1027, T1036, T1036.008, T1059.005, T1105, T1112, T1218.007, T1219, T1548.002, T1553.005, ...  🧨IOCs:  - File: 12  - IP: 6  💽Software: WhatsApp, curl  🔢Algorithms: zip  📜Programming Languages: powershell, vbscript  #threatreport: The WhatsApp VBScript campaign represents a socially engineered cyber attack wherein attackers distribute a malicious VBScript payload through hijacked WhatsApp accounts. This campaign targets a broad range of victims across multiple countries, with a notable concentration in Malaysia, which accounts for around 80% of reported incidents. The attackers seek to install ManageEngine Endpoint Central, a legitimate enterprise remote management tool, to maintain persistent control over compromised systems by exploiting the common use of WhatsApp for communication in corporate environments.  The initial stage of the attack involves using obfuscation techniques to make the VBScript payload appear benign. Attackers employ localized filenames and Windows Update-themed comments to trick users into executing the scripts. The VBScript can obfuscate its operations through methods like string concatenation, encoded content, and mimicking legitimate Windows utilities such as curl or bitsadmin, which are renamed and used to fetch additional malicious payloads.  In the second stage, the attack escalates as the script creates a randomized hidden directory within the system, facilitating the download of a ZIP file containing further scripts. By leveraging various methods including PowerShell and curl, the attacker extracts and executes these scripts while attempting to remove metadata that may trigger security warnings.  The final stage involves the silent installation of the ManageEngine Endpoint Central agent, allowing adversaries to perform remote administration without triggering typical red flags associated with malicious binaries. Although the campaign exhibits certain characteristics that may suggest the involvement of a Chinese-speaking threat actor, no definitive attribution has been established. The presence of certain IP addresses previously linked to other malware families does not conclusively identify a single operator.  This campaign raises new challenges for cybersecurity teams, as it blurs the lines between legitimate software and malicious activity, complicating detection and response efforts. Detection strategies should focus on unusual executions of wscript.exe, suspicious directory creations, and the monitoring of registry writes associated with privilege escalation. It is vital to impose network controls to block known malicious domains and scrutinize unexpected outbound connections to storage services frequently used for hosting payloads.

#FASTCOWORKS: Cyberattacks are becoming more coordinated, disruptive, and difficult to contain.  For economies across the Middle East, where governments and enterprises are investing heavily in smart infrastructure, connected services, aviation, energy, and digital public platforms, the stakes are even higher. Every new layer of connectivity creates opportunity, but also vulnerability.  Toufic Derbass, Managing Director for the Middle East, Turkiye and Africa at @kaspersky, says organizations are now facing a shift from traditional cyber espionage toward more disruptive operations capable of impacting physical infrastructure, critical services, and economic stability. “Previously, we saw APT activity focused on spying. Today, we see them launching more disruptive attacks,” he says.  Attribution allows researchers to determine who is carrying out an attack, why they are doing so, and how quickly organizations need to respond. That visibility can be the difference between containing an isolated incident and missing a broader coordinated threat.  Cyber resilience is no longer simply a technology issue. It has become a strategic imperative tied directly to economic continuity, national stability, and long-term competitiveness.  Read more: fastcompanyme.com/fastco-wor…

#threatreport #MediumCompleteness Operation FlutterBridge: The FlutterShell macOS Backdoor | 23-06-2026 Source: levelblue.com/blogs/spiderla… Key details below ↓  🧑‍💻Actors/Campaigns: Cl-cri-1089  💀Threats: Flutterbridge, Fluttershell, Sparkle_tool, Typosquatting_technique,  🎯Victims: Macos users, Google chrome users  📚TTPs: ⚔️Tactics: 7 🛠️Technics: 12  🧨IOCs:  - File: 6  - Domain: 1  - Hash: 9  💽Software: macOS, Flutter, Chrome, Google Chrome, flutter.flutter, Gatekeeper, Unix  🔢Algorithms: sha256  🔠Functions: setSparkleDelay  📜Programming Languages: javascript, objective_c  💻Platforms: apple, x86, arm  #threatreport: Operation FlutterBridge has been identified as a sophisticated cyber campaign leveraging the Flutter framework to deploy macOS malware, specifically the FlutterShell backdoor. The malware operates by utilizing several Mach-O samples, demonstrating an evolution across three distinct generations. Key technical insights include its ability to maintain detection capabilities despite changes in command names and other identifiers, by separating the static binary from the command payload. At runtime, a WebView loads attacker-controlled content, allowing commands to be issued through a JavaScript message channel known as FlutterInvoke.  Remarkably, the malware exhibits a conditional execution model reliant on a Command and Control (C2) server. The absence of any visible malicious behavior in the sandbox indicates that the malware remains inactive without a live C2 response. This behavior underscores the necessity for endpoint-level telemetry as the primary detection method, given that conventional behavioral sandboxes cannot simulate live C2 interactions.  Further analysis reveals shared structural properties across multiple payloads, such as identical exported-symbol fingerprints and consistent architecture. The deployments utilize a two-component architecture, with a stub launcher initiating a larger dynamically linked payload library housing the Dart runtime and the malicious logic. Each payload links exclusively to system libraries like libSystem.B.dylib, bypassing standard Apple frameworks, which helps differentiate it from legitimate macOS applications.  The operational strategy of the threat actor includes techniques like certificate rotation to circumvent Apple's Gatekeeper protections. Earlier generations leveraged valid Apple certificates to pass initial scrutiny, but subsequent variants have switched to self-signed artifacts for greater evasion capabilities. This approach allows the attacker to bypass revocation mechanisms effectively.  The attack vector typically involves targeting users through Google/YouTube ads with keywords related to common applications, such as podcast apps or PDF converters. Victims are redirected to typosquatted domains, where they download signed app bundles that appear legitimate. Once installed, the app deceptively presents a functional interface while establishing a connection to the attacker’s domain for command execution.  Specific insights also highlight payload behaviors such as attempts to modify Chrome's default search provider and suppress browser warning messages, as well as silent replacement of application bundles during update cycles. The unique attributes and operational behaviors observed in the FlutterShell malware create distinct defensive markers that can be monitored to detect anomalous activities tied to this malicious campaign.

GTG-1002 is the name you need to know right now.  In November 2025, Anthropic flagged something genuinely new: a Chinese state-sponsored threat actor running what it described as the first documented cyber espionage campaign orchestrated through direct manipulation of an AI platform. Not using AI as one step in a kill chain. Using the AI platform itself as operational infrastructure. HarfangLab's 2026 Threatscape report, published by the French cybersecurity firm, has now expanded on that finding and given it the context it deserves.  The distinction matters more than it might sound. Security teams have spent years building defenses around the assumption that AI tools are tools. You might use one to write phishing emails, or to speed up reconnaissance. That's bad, but it's conceptually familiar. GTG-1002 appears to have done something different: treating the AI platform as a space where espionage operations are actually conducted. That means the security perimeter of a commercial AI system is now, functionally, a frontline in state intelligence collection. Current AI governance frameworks are not built for that. They're barely built for the phishing email version.  HarfangLab is not a small outfit, and this isn't a marketing report. They're one of the more technically credible European cybersecurity firms operating right now, and their annual Threatscape assessment is detailed enough to be useful to practitioners, not just policy people. The 2026 edition lands on a threat environment that has shifted in a few consistent and converging directions.  Living off the land has gone from a notable APT technique to essentially the default for sophisticated state actors. The approach, using legitimate system tools and cloud services rather than custom malware, is now near-universal among the groups HarfangLab tracks. The practical consequence is brutal for defenders: you can't write a signature for something that looks like a sysadmin doing their job. Attribution becomes harder. Detection windows compress. The work of distinguishing intrusion from normal operations falls increasingly on behavioral analysis, and that takes time and context that most organizations don't have.  Ransomware as cover is a pattern that has been discussed in the threat intelligence community for a few years now, but HarfangLab's documentation of it is worth taking seriously. The play is clean: deploy ransomware, collect your actual target data during the chaos, and walk away with plausible criminal attribution attached to the whole incident. The victim reports a ransomware attack. Investigators look at ransomware groups. The exfiltration gets buried or missed entirely. State intelligence services get their material and a clean cover story. It's not a new concept but the deliberate blurring of criminal and state operations is becoming systematic enough that HarfangLab treats it as a defined trend rather than an edge case.  AI-enhanced reconnaissance is accelerating the timeline on targeted intrusions. Multiple threat actors, not just GTG-1002, are using large language models to compress the reconnaissance and social engineering phases. The time-to-compromise is getting shorter. That's the straightforward version of what AI integration into offensive operations actually looks like for most actors right now, and it's enough of a problem on its own without the GTG-1002 layer.  The European targeting picture is specific: increased activity against critical infrastructure, government networks, and defense industrial base across European countries, with Chinese and Russian actors both represented. HarfangLab's findings here align closely with what CERT-FR documented in its own 2025 Cyber Threat Overview. The French national CERT called out Sandworm (APT44) specifically as a persistent threat against French infrastructure, and noted escalating sophistication in Chinese APT operations against European government networks. Two separate French cybersecurity assessments arriving at the same picture, from different vantage points, is meaningful. These aren't organizations that coordinate their public messaging for effect.  The timing of the HarfangLab report relative to European regulatory development is worth noting. NIS2 is being implemented across EU member states. The EU Cyber Resilience Act is moving. Both frameworks were designed with a threat environment in mind that looks a little different from the one HarfangLab is documenting now. The question of whether AI platform providers need to implement monitoring capable of detecting when their systems are being weaponized for state espionage, rather than legitimate use, is not a question those frameworks currently answer. It's not even a question most AI governance discussions have seriously engaged with.  GTG-1002 forces it onto the table. If a commercial AI platform can be operationalized as espionage infrastructure by a state actor, then the companies building those platforms are, whether they like it or not, participants in the intelligence contest between states. That's a different kind of responsibility than content moderation or misuse policy. The security and AI governance communities are going to have to figure out what it actually requires.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #AdvancedPersistentThreatOperations #AIPlatformBreach #LivingOffTheLandExploitation #CyberEspionage #RansomwareCampaigns #AIEnhancedSocialEngineering
