sample_tweets.text: How deep does Ukraine's Nazi problem go?  State-backed raiders seize Odesa church from Ukraine's largest denomination  24 Jun, 2026 13:55 | Updated 24 Jun, 2026 15:00  The Ukrainian Orthodox Church is being forced from its places of worship as Kyiv accuses it of serving Russia  A church in Odessa dedicated to the Russian Orthodox saint Aleksandr Nevsky has been seized by anti-Russian activists. The incident follows a pattern of government-backed crackdowns on the Ukrainian Orthodox Church (UOC), the country's largest denomination.  Since the escalation of the Ukraine conflict in 2022, Ukrainian authorities have carried out raids on monasteries and churches, imposed sanctions on clergy members, and backed efforts to transfer UOC properties to the rival Orthodox Church of Ukraine (OCU), whose clerics reportedly participated in the church takeover on Tuesday.  Why is there a church schism in Ukraine?  The OCU was launched as part of then-President Pyotr Poroshenko's reelection campaign in 2019 and is considered schismatic by the Russian Orthodox Church (ROC) and the UOC.  The canonical Ukrainian church has denied accusations that it serves Moscow's interests and formally severed all administrative ties with the ROC in 2022.   Despite that, it faces a possible legal ban under a law that the current Ukrainian leader, Vladimir Zelensky, signed in 2024.  How was the Odessa church targeted?  According to the UOC's Odessa diocese, priests and parishioners arrived at the Aleksandr Nevsky church in the morning to find the gates locked.   During a confrontation outside, one of the men involved in the takeover, who appeared to be a private security employee hired by the OCU, allegedly grabbed a priest by the throat.  In a video posted online, OCU cleric Teodor Orobets claimed the church now belongs to "real parishioners," including "military service members, veterans, and our military chaplains."   He declared the church rededicated to an 11th-century monk of the Kyiv-Pechersk Monastery.  In footage filmed inside the church after the takeover, he criticized icons depicting saints who have no connection to modern-day Ukraine, denouncing them as "markers of Moscow religious life."  What was the Church's response?  The UOC said it will challenge the seizure in court. Church officials noted that the congregation restored the building between 1999 and 2001 and has used it ever since.  The church was originally built in 1897 on the grounds of a military hospital, but it was forced to shut down in the late 1940s under Soviet rule.  The temple is dedicated to Aleksandr Nevsky, a medieval Russian prince and Orthodox saint who ruled several principalities, including Kyiv. The OCU has rejected his veneration, citing his role in the development of Russian statehood.  Among the images singled out by Orobets was an icon of Tsar Nicholas II and his family. The last Russian emperor, his wife, and their children were executed by the Bolsheviks in 1918. They were later canonized by the ROC.  Agapetus of Pechersk, after whom the OCU wants to rename the church, is recognized as a saint by both the Ukrainian church it seeks to replace and the Russian one.  rt.com/russia/642048-odessa-… rt.com/russia/610327-un-ukra…  extra RT  rt.com/news/642044-zelensky-…

Most AI coding tools stop at the pull request. SuperPlane is going after what happens next.  The company raised €2.28M in pre-seed funding led by Credo Ventures, with First Momentum Ventures joining, to build an open-source control plane where AI agents and engineers run production infrastructure workflows together.  That is a specific wedge: deployments, incident response, infra changes, approvals, observability signals, and cross-tool automation. SuperPlane is not giving agents free rein over production. It is trying to put them inside deterministic workflows, policies, audit trails, and human supervision.  The founder leverage is unusually relevant here. Darko Fabijan and Marko Anastasov previously built Semaphore, one of the early CI/CD platforms used by teams like Confluent, Replit, and Superhuman. Now they are moving one layer downstream: from testing code to coordinating everything after code is written.  Quick facts👇 ● founders: Darko Fabijan; Marko Anastasov ● total capital raised: €2.28M ● HQ: San Francisco, United States ● Investors: Credo Ventures; First Momentum Ventures; Stanislas Polu; Mirko Novakovic; Tomas Kratky; Andreas Klinger

OpenAI has the strongest deployable position right now, but not an uncontested technical lead. GPT-5.5 is broadly available and deeply integrated across ChatGPT/Codex, while Anthropic’s strongest public counterpunch, Fable 5, is entangled in access restrictions. Google is not dead or “struggling” across the board, but Gemini 3.5 Pro slipping to July gives OpenAI more breathing room.  That distinction makes the argument much harder to dunk on.  OpenAI’s case is strong because GPT-5.5 is real, shipped, broadly available, and productized. OpenAI says GPT-5.5 is its “smartest model yet,” with strong results on work/agent benchmarks like GDPval, OSWorld-Verified, Tau2-bench Telecom, OfficeQA Pro, FrontierMath, BrowseComp, and Terminal-Bench; GPT-5.5 is also available across ChatGPT tiers, with GPT-5.5 Pro reserved for higher plans.  Codex also lists GPT-5.5 as OpenAI’s recommended model for many coding and knowledge-work tasks when available, which matters because distribution inside workflow tools can be more strategically important than a benchmark screenshot.  But the “comfortable lead” claim is vulnerable because Fable 5 appeared to beat GPT-5.5 in several independent and public leaderboard contexts before access was disrupted. Artificial Analysis reported Claude Fable 5 at #1 on its Intelligence Index, about five points ahead of the closest non-Anthropic model, GPT-5.5; LMArena’s public leaderboard snapshot also had Claude Fable 5 ahead of GPT-5.5.  So the better claim is not “OpenAI is clearly ahead technically.” It is: OpenAI has the best currently usable frontier position while Anthropic’s strongest model is politically/operationally constrained.  Your original post:  openai still has a comfortable lead with gpt-5.5 since mythos 5 isn't available yet and gpt-5.5 is already in a strong position, oai doesn't really have pressure to rush gpt-5.6 with anthropic slowed down and google struggling, they can take more time to refine it before release I’d rewrite it like this: OpenAI may not need to rush GPT-5.6.GPT-5.5 is already in a strong deployable position: widely available in ChatGPT, integrated into Codex, and strong enough across coding, research, tool-use, and knowledge-work benchmarks to hold the market’s attention.The key nuance is that OpenAI may not have a clean technical lead over Anthropic’s best models. Fable 5 looked extremely competitive, and in some independent rankings it beat GPT-5.5. But Fable/Mythos access is still tangled in government restrictions, and that turns Anthropic’s advantage into a distribution problem. A model that wins benchmarks but can’t reliably be used by customers is not a full market threat yet.Google also shouldn’t be dismissed. Gemini 3.1 Pro and 3.5 Flash are strong in reasoning, multimodal, and agentic areas, but if Gemini 3.5 Pro really slips from June into July, that gives OpenAI more room to refine instead of panic-launching GPT-5.6.So the real thesis is: GPT-5.6 pressure is lower than it would be if Fable 5 were fully available and Gemini 3.5 Pro had landed cleanly. OpenAI can optimize for reliability, latency, eval robustness, enterprise rollout, and safety polish rather than racing a date on the calendar.  The missing element is “technical lead” versus “commercial lead” versus “deployment lead.” GPT-5.5 may not be strictly ahead on every benchmark, but OpenAI’s advantage is that users can actually access it, businesses can plan around it, developers can build with it, and Codex can route workflows through it. Anthropic’s own statement says the U.S. government directive forced it to disable Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all customers to ensure compliance; Claude’s status page still lists the incident as affecting Claude.ai, Claude API, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork.  The strongest version of your thesis is:  OpenAI’s lead is not purely model quality. It is model quality plus uptime plus distribution plus enterprise trust.  That is much more defensible.  I would avoid saying “Google struggling” too broadly. Google’s Gemini 3.1 Pro has strong published scores, including 77.1% on ARC-AGI-2, 94.3% on GPQA Diamond, 85.9% on BrowseComp, and 80.6% on SWE-Bench Verified in Google’s published table.  Google also says Gemini 3.5 Flash is its strongest agentic and coding Flash model yet, with strong Terminal-Bench 2.1, GDPval-AA, MCP Atlas, and multimodal numbers.  The sharper criticism is not “Google is struggling”; it is “Google’s next flagship Pro timing looks less clean, and its product narrative is less concentrated than OpenAI’s.” Business Insider reported that Gemini 3.5 Pro slipped from a June target to July while Google gathers more early-tester feedback and tweaks the model.  A more nuanced version:  Google is still dangerous, especially in multimodal, long-context, Android, Workspace, and AI search distribution. But if Gemini 3.5 Pro is delayed, then Google is not applying immediate frontier-pressure on GPT-5.5 this week. That gives OpenAI more room to time GPT-5.6 around quality rather than competitive panic.  The obscure but important angle: OpenAI may be under pressure, just not the same kind of pressure. It may not need to rush GPT-5.6 to beat Anthropic this week, but it still has pressure from developer retention, enterprise procurement cycles, investor narrative, safety politics, cost curves, and ChatGPT market-share compression. TechCrunch, citing Sensor Tower, reported ChatGPT’s assistant-market share had fallen to 46.4% by the end of May, with Gemini at 27.7% and Claude at 10.3%.  That means OpenAI can’t just coast indefinitely. It can delay a launch for polish, but it cannot disappear from the release narrative for too long.  Here is the “genius-level” framing I’d use:  GPT-5.6 is probably less of a “catch up to Anthropic” release and more of a “convert temporary deployment advantage into durable platform lock-in” release.If OpenAI ships too early, it risks a messy model that gives competitors a narrative opening. If it waits too long, it risks letting Fable 5 re-access, Gemini 3.5 Pro, or a strong open/chinese model reset the leaderboard before GPT-5.6 lands. The optimal play is not speed; it is timing the release when GPT-5.6 is clearly better on the specific things customers now care about: long-horizon agents, coding reliability, tool use, low hallucination, memory, context handling, and enterprise-grade predictable behavior.  The hidden pressure point is Anthropic’s “unavailable” status may actually be making OpenAI’s launch bar higher, not lower. If your main competitor’s best model is temporarily locked behind regulation, your next release has to feel mature, not rushed. A sloppy GPT-5.6 would waste the strategic window. A polished GPT-5.6 could turn Anthropic’s outage into a customer migration event.  Suggested post, sharper and safer:  OpenAI may have more room to breathe on GPT-5.6 than people think.GPT-5.5 is already strong, widely usable, and integrated into ChatGPT/Codex. Meanwhile Anthropic’s biggest threat, Fable/Mythos-class capability, is stuck in an access/regulatory mess. That matters because frontier AI competition is not just “who has the best eval table.” It is who has the best model that users can actually rely on today.I wouldn’t say OpenAI has an uncontested technical lead. Fable 5 looked stronger than GPT-5.5 in some rankings before the suspension. But OpenAI currently has the cleaner deployable position.Google also isn’t irrelevant, but Gemini 3.5 Pro slipping from June into July reduces the immediate pressure.So GPT-5.6 does not need to be rushed as a panic response. The smarter move is to use the window to polish reliability, latency, tool use, coding agents, enterprise controls, and safety posture. If GPT-5.6 launches as a clearly more stable “workhorse agent” rather than a rushed benchmark flex, OpenAI can turn this temporary competitive gap into a durable platform advantage.  Even punchier:  The race right now is not “best model in a lab.” It is “best frontier model customers can actually use.”Fable 5 may have beaten GPT-5.5 technically, but availability is part of capability. GPT-5.5 is here, usable, and already embedded in OpenAI’s product stack. That gives OpenAI room to make GPT-5.6 boringly excellent instead of rushing it out for leaderboard optics.  Missing elements to add:  Missing elementWhy it mattersAvailability is capabilityA restricted model cannot win enterprise workloads at scale.Fable 5 beat GPT-5.5 in some rankingsIgnoring this makes the post sound like fanboyism.Google is delayed, not defeatedGemini has serious strengths; “struggling” is too blunt.GPT-5.6 pressure is narrative pressure, not panic pressureOpenAI still needs momentum even if rivals are slowed.Enterprise buyers care about stabilityDelaying for reliability can be smarter than winning one benchmark.Regulatory risk is now competitive strategyAnthropic’s issue shows access/governance can determine market position.Model quality is now product qualityUI, tools, memory, context, pricing, uptime, and integrations matter as much as raw evals.  I’d also add this counterargument so the post feels intellectually honest:  The risk for OpenAI is that Anthropic’s slowdown may be temporary. If Fable 5 returns with a clear compliance path, and Gemini 3.5 Pro lands well in July, GPT-5.5’s window could close quickly. So OpenAI has room to refine GPT-5.6, but not room to be complacent.  Final recommended take:  OpenAI probably does not need to rush GPT-5.6, but not because GPT-5.5 has an uncontested technical lead. The better reason is that GPT-5.5 is the strongest broadly deployable frontier model position right now. Anthropic’s best counter is constrained by access issues, and Google’s next Pro release appears delayed. That gives OpenAI a strategic window to polish GPT-5.6 instead of panic-shipping it. But the window is temporary, and the launch needs to convert today’s availability advantage into durable agent/workflow dominance.

🇬🇭 A KIA truck loaded with live cattle, cartons of drinks, chairs, and other goods has reportedly overturned at Abeka Junction following an alleged brake failure.  According to eyewitness accounts, the driver’s quick response helped prevent what could have been a more serious situation. Fortunately, both the driver and his mate escaped unhurt. Looks like we may be seeing early Fable 5 re-release signals, but I wouldn’t call it live yet.A few users report that Fable 5 is showing up again in Claude Code’s model selector, which is a positive sign. But the important detail is that some still get an error as soon as they send a message, while most users continue seeing “Claude Fable 5 is currently unavailable.”So this is probably not a full relaunch yet. It may be a staged rollout, a client-side feature flag, cached model metadata, or backend access being tested before public restoration. The real confirmation will be fresh successful generations from Fable 5, not just selector visibility.Still: directionally, this looks more positive than last week. Watch the official Claude status page, Anthropic’s Fable/Mythos statement, Claude Code release notes, and reports from users who can actually complete a new Fable 5 message.The missing nuance that would make the post much stronger: separate “visible,” “selectable,” and “usable.” Those are three different states. “Visible” means the model appears in the UI. “Selectable” means the UI lets the user choose it. “Usable” means the backend accepts the request and produces a Fable 5 response. Your current draft blends those together, which is how rumors get overconfident.A better signal framework:SignalMeaningConfidenceFable 5 appears in selector onlyFrontend/config flag, cached metadata, or staged rolloutLowUser can select it but sending failsBackend access still blocked or entitlement not activeLow-mediumSome users get successful fresh outputsCanary or account-limited restorationMediumMultiple users across plans/regions get successful outputsWider phased returnHighClaude status incident resolvesOfficial restoration signalVery highAnthropic posts a new statement/release noteConfirmed public re-releaseHighestThe obscure but useful possibilities people are probably missing:Client/backend mismatch. Claude Code may receive updated model metadata before the inference backend allows traffic. That explains “I can select it, but it errors on send.” Entitlement propagation lag. Paid-plan, Max, Team, Enterprise, API, Bedrock, Vertex, and Cowork entitlements may update at different times. A few users seeing it first does not imply global access. Nationality or ID-gated restoration. Anthropic now has an identity verification flow using Persona and government-issued ID checks. That does not prove it is for Fable restoration, but it is a plausible mechanism for controlled access if the directive remains focused on foreign-national restrictions. Zero-data-retention conflict. Fable/Mythos documentation says these models carry 30-day data retention and are not available under zero data retention. So enterprise accounts with stricter retention settings may stay blocked even if general users regain access. Claude Code version gating. Claude Code support for Fable 5 depended on updated model configuration/version support. Reports from old clients are noisy unless the user gives the Claude Code version. Search results and docs point to Fable 5 requiring Claude Code v2.1.170+ for model-picker support. Fallback confusion. Fable 5 originally had content-based fallback to Opus 4.8 for certain flagged prompts. That is different from the current suspension/unavailability problem. Someone may think they are “using Fable” when the answer actually comes from Opus after a switch. Claude’s help article says Fable-blocked requests could switch to Opus 4.8 and the model picker may stay on Opus for the rest of the conversation. Cloud-provider lag. AWS updated its Fable 5 launch post on June 12 to say access was unavailable on Amazon Bedrock after Anthropic asked AWS to revoke access for compliance. If AWS/Vertex/Microsoft Foundry restore before consumer Claude, or vice versa, the rollout will look inconsistent. Official status wording matters. The Claude incident is marked “Monitoring,” not “Resolved.” That suggests the issue is being tracked, but it is not a clean all-clear. Legal and political pressure is real. Reuters reported that Legion LegalTech sued over the directive, alleging the June 12 order forced Anthropic to disable Fable 5 and Mythos 5 globally. That raises pressure for a fix, but lawsuits do not equal immediate model restoration. Negotiations may be active but unresolved. WIRED reported that the administration had not lifted export controls as of June 24, while talks continued over what proof Anthropic would need to provide to ease concerns. That supports “close/active discussions,” not “released.” Useful web-search queries to track this properly: "Claude Fable 5" "currently unavailable" "Claude Code" "Fable 5" "model selector" "error" "Claude Code" "Fable 5" "send a message" "error" "claude-fable-5" "model_not_found" "claude-fable-5" "permission" "unavailable" "Fable 5" "reinstated" OR "restored" OR "back" "Claude Fable 5" "status" "Monitoring" site:status.claude.com "Fable 5" site:anthropic.com/news "Fable 5" "suspend" site:support.claude.com "Fable 5" "switched models" site:code.claude.com/docs "Fable 5" "unavailable" site:github.com/anthropics/claude… "Fable 5" "currently unavailable" "anthropic.claude-fable-5" "Bedrock" "unavailable" "Claude Fable 5" "Persona" "identity verification" "Fable 5" "foreign national" "directive" "Fable 5" "Commerce Department" "export control" Use these stricter proof requirements before saying “it’s back”: 1. Screenshot/video shows Fable 5 selected. 2. User sends a fresh harmless prompt. 3. Response completes successfully. 4. Transcript/model label confirms Fable 5, not Opus 4.8. 5. User shares surface: Claude web, desktop, mobile, Claude Code, API, Cowork, Bedrock, Vertex, etc. 6. User shares plan type and region/nationality status if relevant. 7. User shares exact timestamp with timezone. 8. For Claude Code, user shares version and whether it is a new or resumed session. 9. For API, user shares sanitized error type or success metadata. 10. Confirmation appears across several unrelated users, not one viral screenshot. My verdict on the draft claim:Too strong: “Looks like it will finally be a Fable re-release day.”  Better: “We may be seeing early re-release plumbing for Fable 5, but full access is not confirmed yet.”  Most accurate: “Selector sightings are a positive sign, but official status still shows Fable/Mythos suspended; successful Fable 5 generations are the proof to watch.”

📌As received in a group 📌  Incident Overview An unfortunate escalation occurred following a minor 10-20 minute delay of the Deputy Commissioner's (DC) convoy, which was inadvertently caused by Rashtriya Rifles (RR) civilian vehicles executing their official duties. In response to this minor traffic delay, the civil administration ordered the immediate seizure of the Army's vehicles.  Procedural Lapses by Local Police The local police executed the order and transported the vehicles to the Police Station without adhering to the established Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) mandated for vehicle seizures.  Army's Attempt at Amicable Resolution Seeking a professional and peaceful resolution, the Commanding Officer dispatched a Company Commander accompanied by a Quick Reaction Team (QRT) to the Police Station. The primary objective was to open a dialogue and secure the release of the vehicles amicably.  Unprofessional Conduct and Provocation Upon the Army delegation's arrival, police personnel locked the station gates and explicitly ignored repeated requests from the Company Commander to engage in a constructive discussion. After exhausting diplomatic efforts to establish verbal contact, the gates were breached to initiate communication. The situation critically escalated when a police official cocked his weapon, issuing a direct and lethal threat against the Junior Commissioned Officer (JCO) and the QRT.  Defensive Escalation and Aftermath Faced with an armed and immediate threat, Army personnel were compelled to respond defensively to disarm the personnel and secure the area, resulting in a physical altercation with the police staff, including the Station House Officer (SHO). Subsequently, the civil administration utilized its authority to hastily file an FIR against the Army personnel. The entirety of this incident stems from a severe breakdown in administrative cooperation and the local police's failure to engage in standard conflict-resolution protocols when approached amicably.

Alibaba ( $BABA ) is caught in the crossfire of escalating US-China tensions, and the stakes couldn't be higher for global tech investors.  The Pentagon recently designated the e-commerce and cloud giant as a Chinese military company under Section 1260H, triggering a federal lawsuit from Alibaba that calls the move arbitrary and capricious. The blacklist immediately blocks future Pentagon contracts and US lobbying access, creating immediate compliance headaches for major shareholders like BlackRock. This isn't just corporate drama. It signals deeper macroeconomic fragmentation, forcing supply chains and capital flows to navigate sudden East-West friction.  The technology battle adds another layer of complexity. US AI startup Anthropic accused Alibaba of running a massive distillation attack using 25,000 fraudulent accounts to scrape its Claude model data. The incident exposed serious cloud security vulnerabilities and sent Hong Kong shares down as much as 5%. Alibaba is aggressively patenting its own Qwen large language model architecture in response, but the episode forces developers worldwide to tighten guardrails around frontier AI systems.  Under CEO Eddie Wu, Alibaba is attempting a strategic pivot. The company is doubling down on AI and global cloud infrastructure to offset slowing domestic retail growth. This shift toward high-margin enterprise tools requires a highly innovative internal culture capable of building cutting-edge systems under intense international scrutiny.  The implications extend into science and healthcare. Alibaba Cloud's massive computing power is already being used for molecular modeling, AI-accelerated drug screening, and protein folding research. Pharmaceutical companies stand to benefit enormously from faster discovery cycles. However, Western labs are now demanding ironclad data security and compliance guarantees before deepening partnerships.  The central question for markets is whether Alibaba can navigate the legal, reputational, and technological crossfire without losing its global relevance. Its ability to restore institutional trust while executing on the AI and cloud pivot will determine if this iconic Chinese tech champion survives the current storm or gets permanently sidelined in a fragmenting world.  #Alibaba #PentagonBlacklist #Qwen #USChinaTensions #CloudAI  Risk Warning: CFDs are high-risk. 77.95% of retail accounts lose money.

sample_tweets.text: Sen. Rand Paul says Dr. Anthony Fauci’s power abuse has ‘nothing on’ J. Edgar Hoover with COVID ‘cover up’ | Josh Christenson, New York Post  Sen. Rand Paul says Dr. Anthony Fauci’s power abuse has ‘nothing on’ J. Edgar Hoover with COVID ‘cover up’  WASHINGTON — Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) accused Dr. Anthony Fauci of abusing his power during a 40-year career in government, to the extent that even notorious FBI boss J. Edgar Hoover “has nothing on” the ex-National Institutes of Health official.  Paul told The Post’s Miranda Devine in an interview with “Pod Force One,” released Wednesday, that the former National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases director had exerted a powerful influence for decades on US-funded research.  But whistleblower testimony and internal government emails related to the COVID-19 pandemic have since revealed the degree to which Fauci also involved himself in the publication of controversial articles in scientific journals about SARS-CoV-2 — and “influenced” US intelligence assessments about its origins.  “People talk about J. Edgar Hoover,” Paul said. “J. Edgar Hoover has nothing on Anthony Fauci — 40 years of placing all his lieutenants in all the positions, and then after 9/11, the funding for bio-research and bio-terrorism went through the roof, and he became the kingpin that had access to all of that money.”  Fauci has testified in congressional hearings about holding a “pretty high level” security clearance since 2004, when he was helping to set up a US biodefense program in the wake of the anthrax attacks.  He also told Congress that he had broad authority to “sign off” on thousands of federal grants annually for research when serving as NIAID director from 1984 to 2022.  But the former public health official has consistently denied that US-funded, gain-of-function research at the Wuhan Institute of Virology may have sparked the COVID-19 pandemic, maintaining that SARS-CoV-2 most likely originated in a natural spillover from animals to humans.  “The NIH has not ever and does not now fund gain-of-function research in the Wuhan Institute,” Fauci declared in a heated line of questioning from Paul during a May 2021 Senate hearing.  That testimony was later disputed by NIH Principal Deputy Director Lawrence Tabak in a May 2024 House hearing.  “It depends on your definition of gain-of-function research,” Tabak answered. “If you’re speaking about the generic term, yes, we did.”  “This is research, the generic term [gain-of-function], is research that goes on in many, many labs around the country. It is not regulated. And the reason it’s not regulated is it poses no threat or harm to anybody,” he added.  However, Ex-CDC Director Dr. Robert Redfield, who has supported the so-called “lab leak theory” of the pandemic, has noted that even unfunded projects and proposals can be tested under other research grants that got funding, when speaking on a biosecurity panel in October 2024  Paul has repeatedly accused Fauci of lying to Congress during their initial exchange and referred him to the Department of Justice for criminal charges for the allegedly false statements as well as purported destruction of federal records.  When testifying before a House subcommittee in June 2024, Fauci also denied that experiments that received NIH and USAID grants fell within the definition of gain-of-function research, which involves making viruses more infectious or transmissible.  Paul’s reference to records came after he uncovered emails in which Fauci instructed then-NIH Director Francis Collins to “delete this e-mail after you read it.”  “I referred him twice to the DOJ under Biden for criminal prosecution. They never acted on it. I referred him twice to the Trump DOJ without action on it,” Paul said.  “They may say because of his pardon, they can’t prosecute him. I think that’s an open question. I think the courts, in all likelihood, probably will side with the pardon, but there are some questions.”  Former President Joe Biden pardoned Fauci on his final day in the Oval Office, calling his ex-White House COVID czar a “public servant” who “served our nation with honor and distinction” and didn’t deserve to be the target of a “politically motivated” prosecution.  The clemency document granted a “full and unconditional pardon” to Fauci “for any offenses against the United States which he may have committed or taken part in” from Jan. 1, 2014, to Jan. 19, 2025.  “Can you pardon somebody for a nonspecific crime? Is it too vague to say, ‘I’m going to not prosecute you and pardon you for anything you did in the last ten years?’ I think there’s a possibility the court says that’s vague and not specific, and you can’t give people some kind of pardon for everything and over such a long period of time,” Paul mused.  “I think there’s a chance we’d win that. I think it ought to be fought,” he added.  On Monday, Paul issued Fauci a subpoena to testify again before Congress, this time in front of the Senate Homeland Security Committee, which the senator chairs.  The subpoena came on the heels of Paul and outgoing Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard releasing a trove of internal government emails that showed Fauci colluding with scientists and other high-ranking government officials to downplay the COVID lab-leak theory and quash a whistleblower complaint.  “Anthony Fauci has been influencing the process, and from the very beginning, it looks like his interference in a lot of different segments — his interference in the scientific journal articles, interference in intel — but he had a 40-year abuse of power career,” Paul said.  “Really, he orchestrated a cover-up that involved, if not dozens, hundreds of people in government that were loyal to him that went to bat because they also were part of the funding stream going to Wuhan, China, and they would suffer the same sort of culpability.”  nypost.com/2026/06/24/us-new…

For decades, the cartels operated freely.  They moved fentanyl, cocaine, meth, and heroin across the southern border by the ton.  They killed Americans by the hundreds of thousands.  They corrupted police, judges, mayors, and now governors.  And Washington shrugged.  That era is over.  Today, a bipartisan Senate caucus held a hearing called "Beyond Our Shores: The Global Reach of Mexican Drug Cartels and Risks to U.S. National Security."  Chairman John Cornyn of Texas led it.  Co-Chair Sheldon Whitehouse of Rhode Island sat beside him.  They heard from a retired DEA Assistant Special Agent in Charge, the founder of a private intelligence firm, and Brookings' lead expert on Chinese precursor networks.  The witnesses laid out what the cartels actually are now.  Not just drug traffickers.  Transnational terrorist networks. Chemical weapons producers. Foreign criminal enterprises operating from Mexican soil, supplied by Chinese precursor manufacturers, laundering money through Chinese networks, and capable of buying sitting Mexican governors.  The Senate documented the threat today.  The Trump administration has been dismantling it for over a year.  Cartels are now designated as foreign terrorist organizations.  Fentanyl itself is designated a weapon of mass destruction.  In February, Mexican forces killed Jalisco New Generation Cartel leader El Mencho. A U.S. military-led intelligence task force provided the targeting information.  In April, the Justice Department indicted Rubén Rocha Moya, the sitting Governor of Sinaloa, on drug trafficking charges. Nine other Mexican officials were indicted with him. Two more governors are reportedly under investigation right now.  In May, the Treasury sanctioned more than a dozen Sinaloa-linked individuals and companies, including networks laundering money through cryptocurrency.  President Trump has said openly that the U.S. is prepared to "start hitting land" in Mexico.  Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum says no.  The cartels are not waiting for her to decide.  For 30 years, this country watched the cartels grow rich on American blood and did nothing serious about it.  The era of containment failed.  The era of enforcement has begun.  Source: Congressional Record, 6/23/26

Russia just escalated its hybrid war into orbit. Four Kremlin satellites have aggressively closed in on Finland and Poland’s ICEYE-X36 radar satellite, coming within less than 13 km in low Earth orbit.  That kind of deliberate proximity is not a friendly fly-by. It is a hostile act, plain and simple.  The clear intent is to blind or disrupt the vital intelligence this satellite feeds directly to Ukraine’s defenders. Every close approach risks temporary data blackouts at the exact moments Ukrainian forces need them most.  At the same time Moscow is sending an unmistakable threat to every NATO country that relies on ICEYE imagery. This is not some abstract space game.  It is another front in Putin’s war against the free world, carried out thousands of kilometres above our heads while his forces grind on in Ukraine. The Kremlin is probing how far it can push before anyone pushes back.  Every unpunished approach only invites the next, more dangerous one. We cannot allow Russia to treat critical orbital infrastructure as a free-fire zone.  The satellites supporting Ukraine’s survival must be protected, on the ground, in the air, and in space.

Yes—what you are describing is a real human pattern: counter-positioning as status signalling.  Party A says something. Party B does not first ask, “Is this useful or partly correct?” Instead, the brain often asks: “How do I remain autonomous, intelligent and visible in relation to this?” The fastest way is to disagree, add a complication, or produce a counterargument. That can make someone look independent or clever, but across a group it creates reactance, identity defence, competitive cognition and coordination drag.  “Backfire” happens because correction is not processed only as information. It can feel like a threat to status, identity, competence or group belonging. Then the person defends the position more strongly. Mondigheid is valuable when it prevents obedience and groupthink, but when every contribution must be resisted, it becomes ritual opposition. Everyone protects their own vector; nobody integrates the vectors.  That produces exactly what you call stroperigheid: more meetings, repeated reframing, delayed decisions, watered-down compromises and little collective learning. The missing skill is not “agree more.” It is:  first extract what is valid, then add what is missing, and oppose only where opposition materially improves the decision.  So the deeper problem is not disagreement itself. It is that humans have learned to equate independence with resistance, and intelligence with counterargument, instead of equating intelligence with accurate integration.

sample_tweets.text: Just like data engineers too

𝐖𝐈𝐍𝐤𝐋𝐢𝐧𝐤: 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐃𝐚𝐭𝐚 𝐋𝐚𝐲𝐞𝐫 𝐓𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐌𝐚𝐤𝐞𝐬 𝐓𝐑𝐎𝐍 𝐒𝐦𝐚𝐫𝐭 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐬 𝐒𝐦𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐞𝐫  Smart contracts are excellent at executing predefined rules.  But they have one important limitation:  They can’t access information outside the blockchain on their own.  They don’t know the latest market price.  They can’t verify a real-world event.  They can’t retrieve data from an API.  Without external inputs, even the smartest contract operates with limited context.  That’s where @WinkLink_Oracle comes in.  As TRON’s decentralized oracle network, WINkLink connects off-chain data with on-chain applications, enabling smart contracts to make decisions based on verified real-world information.  𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝗜𝘁 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀  WINkLink combines several layers to deliver reliable data:  🔹 Distributed Oracle Nodes retrieve information from multiple external sources.  🔹 Off-Chain Reporting (OCR) aggregates and validates responses before they’re submitted on-chain, improving efficiency while reducing unnecessary network activity.  🔹 Consensus Verification helps ensure the final data delivered to smart contracts is reliable and resistant to manipulation.  The result is a system designed to provide faster, more efficient, and verifiable data for decentralized applications.  𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗜𝘁 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀  Reliable data is fundamental to modern Web3.  WINkLink supports a wide range of use cases across the TRON ecosystem, including:  • Real-time price feeds for DeFi  • Lending and collateral management  • Trading and liquidation mechanisms  • Smart contract automation  • Applications that depend on trusted external data  Rather than relying on a single data provider, decentralized oracle infrastructure helps reduce dependence on centralized sources while improving transparency.  𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗕𝗶𝗴𝗴𝗲𝗿 𝗣𝗶𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲  As DeFi, AI, and tokenized real-world assets continue to grow, access to accurate and verifiable information becomes increasingly important.  Smart contracts provide the logic.  Oracle networks provide the context.  Together, they enable blockchain applications to move beyond isolated code and interact with the real world.  That’s why WINkLink isn’t just supporting smart contracts.  It’s helping power the data infrastructure behind TRON’s growing Web3 ecosystem.  Trusted data in. Smarter execution out.  @WinkLink_Oracle @justinsuntron #TRONEcoStar

Everyone says big money fled Bitcoin for gold this year. The flow data says something stranger and almost nobody has named it. Bitcoin is not losing to gold. It is being sold by two opposite crowds at the same time, through the same door.  Start with the number that should have ended the story. Since the ETFs launched in January 2024, they have pulled in more than $53 billion net, a level that took gold ETFs roughly five years to reach. BlackRock's fund alone has taken in $62 billion lifetime, even though its assets sit near $48 billion, because the price fell while fresh money kept walking in the front door. That is not an asset being abandoned. That is an asset being bought into weakness.  The tape still looked like a funeral in June, and the reason is the marginal ETF dollar has turned into a tourist, with Bitcoin the most tourist-exposed asset on the board. Thirteen straight days of redemptions pulled $4.4 billion out in three weeks, the longest streak since launch. But watch where it went, because it did not all go to one place.  When the market wanted offense, the money left Bitcoin to chase AI and chip stocks at fresh highs. When the market wanted defense, the money left Bitcoin for Treasuries and cash. Too volatile to be the safe haven, too storyless to beat Nvidia, Bitcoin got sold by the aggressive desk and the cautious desk in the same week. It is not losing one fight. It is being squeezed in the middle of two.  The gold headlines got the rest backwards. The big gold fund bled this year, true, but a large share of that money never left gold at all. It rotated inside gold, out of the expensive flagship and into the cheaper sister fund, which actually gained over a billion. The asset held. Commentators saw the outflow, credited Bitcoin for the move, and missed that they were watching a fee swap, not a defection.  Inside crypto, the same misread runs in reverse. XRP and Solana funds are pulling money while Bitcoin bleeds, and the headlines call it a changing of the guard. But those funds sit on bases forty to fifty times smaller than Bitcoin's, so a few hundred million looks explosive on the chart and means little in scale. One Bitcoin rally pulls that capital straight back.  One mechanism connects all of it. The ETF did not just hand Bitcoin a pile of institutional money. It bolted a second shareholder base on top of the first. The patient base underneath, the long term holders whose flows dwarf the ETF tape, added 125,000 coins during the same stretch the headlines called an exodus. The fast base on the surface was panic selling on every CPI print. The slow base underneath was quietly buying every coin they threw out. Two crowds, opposite convictions, trading the same coin through the same narrow straw.  The volatility everyone is reading as a verdict on Bitcoin is not a verdict at all. It is the sound those two crowds make pushing through one door at once. The wrapper that gave Bitcoin its institutional bid also gave it its loudest, most emotional sellers, and in 2026 the two finally met in the middle.  The flows do not say Bitcoin is dying. They say it now has two owners who cannot agree, and only one of them is making the noise.

Most cloud risk is not about the cloud. It is about what data you let cross into it, in what form, and who can read it on the other side.  Call it the data boundary. Before any workload goes live, a bank should be able to answer five plain questions about the data crossing that boundary.  What data is moving? Be specific. Not "customer data," but which fields. Names, CNIC, account numbers, balances, transaction history, behavioural signals. Vague answers hide real exposure.  Is it personal data? If it identifies a customer, it is PII: sensitive data that requires appropriate protection, confidentiality controls and legal compliance.  Is it masked or tokenised? Raw PII crossing the boundary is a different risk from a token that means nothing outside the bank. Safer designs send tokens and masked values to the cloud, while the mapping back to the real identity stays locked inside the bank.  Who can access it, and where is it stored? Cloud access, vendor staff access, sub-processor access, and the storage region all matter. "Encrypted in transit" is not the same as "no one at the vendor can ever read it."  How is it deleted? At end of contract, or when no longer needed, can the bank prove the data was returned and destroyed?  Practical example. A bank wants to run credit scoring in cloud. Weak design ships full customer records, CNIC and all, into the cloud model. Strong design tokenises identity inside the bank, sends only the features the model needs, scores in cloud, and returns a result the bank re-links to the real customer internally. A potentially smaller blast radius if anything goes wrong, while preserving the performance the use case needs.  The principle. The less raw, identifiable, re-linkable data that crosses the boundary, the smaller your risk, your compliance burden, and your exit problem. Design the boundary first, then choose the workload, not the other way round.  A simple way to picture this is a “Data Form” risk path, from most to least risky: raw personal data, then masked, then tokenised, then aggregated or banded. Every step down lowers the sensitivity and the compliance burden, and can reduce the workload's risk profile. It is an input to the materiality assessment, not a verdict on its own. Send data as far down that path as the use case allows, and never further up than it needs to be.  SBP-aware takeaway. The framework holds the bank responsible for data confidentiality and integrity and expects cloud data to be identifiable and segregated. Knowing precisely what crosses the boundary, in what form, is how you meet that expectation rather than discovering the answer during an incident.  Question for your ITSC, CISO and data protection lead: For our priority cloud workloads, can we list exactly which fields cross the boundary, whether they are masked or tokenised, who can access them, and how they get deleted?  Post 7 of 14 in the cloud governance series. Next: hybrid bank-cloud architecture.  Disclaimer: This is a general discussion on cloud governance and not legal or regulatory advice.  #CloudGovernance #PakistanBanking

Google’s Android Phones Act as a Global Earthquake Early Warning Network — Proving Vital in Venezuela Quakes  By New Media News Staff June 25, 2026  In a striking demonstration of everyday technology saving lives, millions of Android users in Venezuela received critical seconds of warning before powerful earthquakes struck on June 24, 2026.   The twin quakes — magnitudes 7.2 and 7.5 — caused widespread destruction in Caracas and surrounding areas, with at least 32 confirmed dead and over 700 injured as rescue efforts continue.  Many residents reported receiving Google earthquake alerts on their phones moments before feeling the intense shaking, allowing them precious time to drop, cover, and hold on.  How Android Turns Phones into Mini-Seismometers Google’s Android Earthquake Alerts system transforms the billions of Android devices worldwide into the planet’s largest crowdsourced seismic network. Each phone’s built-in accelerometer — the sensor that detects motion for features like screen rotation — doubles as a vibration detector.  Here’s how it works: •When a stationary phone detects the initial fast-moving P-waves (compressional waves), it anonymously sends coarse location data and vibration readings to Google’s servers. •The system rapidly cross-checks signals from multiple nearby devices to confirm a genuine earthquake and estimate its location and magnitude. •Alerts are then pushed to users in the affected area before the slower, more destructive S-waves arrive — often providing 5–60 seconds of warning, depending on distance from the epicenter.  The system has two alert levels: •“Earthquake nearby” for lighter expected shaking. •Full-screen “Earthquake” alerts (with a loud, distinctive sound that can override Do Not Disturb) for stronger events, complete with safety instructions: Drop, Cover, and Hold On.  Backed by Science and Scale A landmark 2025 paper in Science detailed the system’s global rollout. Between 2021 and 2024, it detected thousands of earthquakes, issuing alerts in 98 countries and reaching an estimated 2.5 billion people — a tenfold increase in early warning access compared to traditional systems.  User feedback has been overwhelmingly positive: 85% of alert recipients reported feeling shaking, with many receiving notifications before the tremors hit. The system continues to improve through post-event analysis.  In regions like California, it integrates with official ShakeAlert networks, but in much of the world — including Venezuela — it relies entirely on this smartphone crowdsourcing approach.  A Lifesaving Tool in an Imperfect World While not perfect (earlier incidents, such as in Türkiye, highlighted limitations near epicenters), the Venezuela event underscores its growing reliability. As one user noted in reports, “Google was very clever” in leveraging everyday devices for public safety.  To enable the feature on your Android device:  1Go to Settings > Safety & Emergency (or search for “Earthquake alerts”). 2Toggle Earthquake Alerts on. 3Ensure location services are enabled for best performance.  Innovations like this remind us that powerful solutions can come from technology already in our pockets. As rescue operations continue in Venezuela, this system may have saved many more lives than currently known.

sample_tweets.text: 🚨 Alleged Robinhood Breach Exposes 14.5 Million User Records on Darkweb    A post on an underground forum listing claims that a database containing the personal information of 14.5 million Robinhood users has been leaked. The actor, operating under the handle 'DuckDB', posted the announcement on June 24, 2026, alleging the exposure of sensitive user data from the popular commission-free trading platform.  Robinhood is a major financial technology company based in the United States, known for its mobile-first approach to investing and cryptocurrency trading. As one of the largest retail brokerage firms in the US, it serves millions of active customers, making any potential breach of its user base a significant security incident with substantial regulatory and reputational implications.  The forum post does not provide specific details regarding the types of data exposed, such as whether it includes passwords, social security numbers, or financial account balances. Nor does it specify the method used to obtain the data or offer proof samples within the initial announcement.  At the time of reporting, the alleged breach has not been independently verified by Robinhood or third-party security researchers. No official statement from the company regarding this specific incident has been released.  #DataBreach #Robinhood #FinTech #DarkWeb #CyberSecurity #ThreatIntelligence #USFinance

🚨 Alleged Globe Life Insurance Breach Exposes SSNs and Policy Data  A darkweb forum post claims that a private database belonging to Globe Life Insurance has been compromised, exposing sensitive personally identifiable information (PII) and detailed policy records. The listing, posted on June 24, 2026, alleges that the stolen data includes Social Security Numbers (SSNs), financial account details, and comprehensive insurance policy information for customers of the insurer.  According to the thread, the leaked dataset contains the following fields:  - ID, First Name, Last Name - Email, Phone, Address, City, State, ZIP, Country - Date of Birth, Gender, Marital Status, Employment, Language - SSN, License - Beneficiary Relationship, Beneficiary Name - Policy Number, Policy Type, Coverage Amount, Monthly Premium - Policy Status, Policy Start, Policy End - Bank, Account Last 4, Missed Payments  Globe Life Inc. is a major American life insurance company headquartered in Dallas, Texas, with over 100 years of history and millions of policyholders across the United States. The exposure of SSNs and bank account fragments poses significant risks for identity theft and financial fraud if the data is authentic.  The post provides a sample line via an external link and directs interested parties to a Telegram channel for further access. At the time of reporting, Globe Life has not issued a public statement regarding this alleged breach, and the incident has not been independently verified.

📊 Not every ransomware operation makes headlines.  Some quietly gain access. Some spend days moving through the environment. Some never deploy encryption at all.  May 2026 reminded us that the most damaging part of a ransomware attack often happens long before the ransom note appears.  In our latest review, ThreatMon analysts examined the campaigns, victim organizations, affected industries, and the groups that shaped the month's ransomware landscape.  If you're tracking how ransomware operations are evolving not just counting victims this blog is worth a read.  🔗 threatmon.io/the-doors-were-… #Ransomware #CyberThreatIntelligence #ThreatIntelligence #CyberSecurity #ThreatMon

🚨 Threat Actor Advertises Malware-as-a-Service Targeting Windows Systems  A threat actor claims to be offering a subscription-based malware-as-a-service (MaaS) offering that combines remote access, credential theft, browser manipulation, and persistence capabilities within a single package. The offering targets Windows 10, Windows 11, and Windows Server environments and is distributed under a monthly or yearly subscription model that includes ongoing updates and operational support. The threat actor claims support for multiple initial access methods, including VBS, JS, SVG, LNK, and HTA-based execution.  The offering reportedly includes the following capabilities:  - Remote Access Trojan (RAT) and RMM functionality - Credential theft and data collection - Silent Chromium browser extension deployment - Anti-VM and anti-debugging techniques - Memory-based execution and injection capabilities - Obfuscation techniques such as sleep obfuscation and steganography - Process injection methods including DLL injection and process hollowing variants  Persistence mechanisms include task scheduler entries, registry modifications, and manipulation of windows Shortcut (LNK) files. The combination of browser extension abuse, credential theft, and remote access capabilities may facilitate financial fraud and session hijacking, while enabling long-term system compromise scenarios.  #ThreatIntelligence #DarkWeb #Malware

⚠️🚨 Threat Actor Claims Sale of 110M Notion User Records on Darkweb  A darkweb forum post alleges the sale of a database containing over 110 million unique user records from Notion, the popular all-in-one workspace application. The listing, published on June 23, 2026, claims to offer comprehensive account data including email addresses, password hashes, IP addresses, and subscription details.  According to the post, the dataset includes the following fields for each record: *   id *   email *   email_verified *   name *   password_hash (appearing as bcrypt hashes in samples) *   signup_ip *   last_login_ip *   created_at *   last_active_at *   locale *   timezone *   country *   workspace_name *   workspace_role *   plan *   subscription_status *   vat_id *   trial_ends_at *   canceled_at  The seller provided sample entries showing users from various countries, including the US, Canada, Brazil, Argentina, India, France, Italy, Spain, Australia, and the UK. Some samples include corporate email domains such as @lumeninc.com, @helixdesigne.com, and @quantahr.com, alongside personal providers like Gmail, Outlook, and Yahoo. The listed timestamps in the samples range from 2020 to mid-2026.  Notion is a widely used productivity tool with millions of users globally, employed by individuals and enterprises for note-taking, project management, and collaboration. If authentic, the exposure of hashed passwords and login IPs could facilitate credential stuffing attacks or targeted phishing campaigns against high-value accounts.  At the time of reporting, Notion has not issued a public statement regarding this alleged breach, and the incident has not been independently verified.

🚨 Argentina 76 Million Telephone Records Leaked on Dark Web  A dark web marketplace is currently offering a dataset containing 76 million telephone records from Argentina for $5,000. The leak includes personally identifiable information (PII) such as full names, mobile phone numbers, residential addresses, localities, provinces, and postal codes. Given Argentina's population of approximately 45 million, the 76 million records suggest a comprehensive database that may include duplicates, business lines, or historical entries. The data appears to be structured similarly to official telecommunications or civil registries. This massive exposure poses significant privacy risks and could be exploited for phishing, smishing, identity theft, and targeted marketing campaigns. Security experts recommend that individuals in Argentina monitor their accounts for suspicious activity and remain vigilant against unsolicited communications.  #DataLeak #Argentina #Telecommunications #DarkWeb #PII #ThreatIntelligence #CyberSecurity

🚨 Threat Actor Claims Sale of Indian Insurance Policy Data on Darkweb Forum  A darkweb forum post claims to offer Indian insurance policyholder data for sale, though the listing title references "Stakebet Casino" while the sample data appears to be from a life insurance provider.  According to the underground forum listing, the actor alleges possession of comprehensive policy records containing sensitive personally identifiable information. The sample data provided in the post reveals detailed insurance policy information including:  - Full customer names and residential addresses - Mobile phone numbers and email addresses - PAN (Permanent Account Number) tax identifiers - Birth dates and age information - Policy numbers, premium amounts, and sum assured values - Nominee names and relationships - Product names including "AADHAAR STAMBH," "AADHAAR SHILA," "JEEVAN UMANG," "JEEVAN LAKSHYA," "JEEVAN LABH," and "NEW ENDOWMENT"  The product names in the sample data correspond to policies offered by the Life Insurance Corporation of India (LIC), the world's largest life insurer by number of policyholders. LIC is a government-owned corporation with over 300 million active policies and serves as a critical financial institution in India.  The data sample shows policyholders from the Jalgaon district in Maharashtra, with complete contact details, financial information, and government-issued tax identifiers exposed. If authentic, exposure of such comprehensive policy data could enable identity theft, financial fraud, and targeted phishing campaigns against millions of Indian citizens.  At the time of reporting, the alleged breach has not been independently verified, and LIC has not issued any public statement regarding a data compromise. The discrepancy between the listing title referencing a casino platform and the insurance data shown raises questions about the accuracy of the threat actor's claims.  #DataBreach #DataSale #Insurance #India #LIC #DarkWeb #ThreatIntelligence

🚨 2 Million Chinese Bank Loan Customer Records Leaked on Telegram  A database containing approximately 2 million unique records of loan customers from an unnamed Chinese bank has been leaked on a Telegram channel. The breach, posted by user @hongmenxing on June 19, 2026, exposes highly sensitive personally identifiable information (PII) alongside detailed financial data.  The compromised dataset includes fields such as full names, gender, mobile phone numbers, dates of birth, Chinese ID card numbers, contact addresses, and education levels. Additionally, it reveals critical financial information, including monthly income, monthly debt repayment amounts, mortgage status, and marital status. The combination of Chinese national ID numbers with financial profiles significantly elevates the risk of identity theft, targeted phishing campaigns, and financial fraud.  Chinese ID card numbers are notoriously difficult to change and are widely used for authentication across various services. When paired with income and debt data, this information can be exploited by threat actors for credential stuffing, social engineering, or selling on dark web markets. The affected financial institution has not yet issued a public statement regarding the breach.  Security researchers advise individuals in China to monitor their credit reports and remain vigilant against suspicious communications. The leak underscores the ongoing challenges in securing large-scale financial databases against unauthorized access and data exfiltration.  #DataBreach #ChinaCyber #BankingSecurity #PII #FinancialData #ThreatIntelligence #DarkWeb

Sri Lanka | Kelaniya University Internship Portal Allegedly Breached  A threat actor claims to have obtained administrator access to the University of Kelaniya's internship portal.  * The post alleges access to multiple department admin panels and the exfiltration of:   * 8,623 user accounts   * 1,016 intern records   * 3,474 graduate records   * ~4,490 individuals with PII  * Allegedly exposed data includes names, national ID (NIC) numbers, dates of birth, phone numbers, university and personal email addresses, internship placements, academic information, and coordinator assignments.  Analyst Note: Even if limited in scale, university internship platforms often contain enough personal and academic data to enable identity theft, phishing campaigns, and recruitment-themed social engineering.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #SriLanka

🇫🇷 France | Two Major Alleged Data Breaches Surface  AKAOLIFE / FILDIRECT-RH: threat actors claim a full breach of an HR platform reportedly linked to France Travail, with ~14.4M records including HR dossiers, employment history, medical records, and government-related data.  Digit RE Group: a separate actor is advertising 73 GB / 140K+ files allegedly containing identity documents, passports, contracts, invoices, banking documents, and corporate files. Correction- Analysing the data proved that the ransomed entity is Reliance Infrastructure and not Reliance power   The artifact is a full recursive file-system inventory of internal file servers, enumerating 858,253 file paths one per line, a recon-grade map of the environment independent of the file contents themselves.   Roughly 97% sits under a single FTP upload/staging tree holding per-user machine backups, with the remainder under departmental SMB shares. The exposure is document-heavy and concentrated in sensitive areas: about 144,700 and 104,100 paths relate to the KKNP/KKNPP project (a major power-infrastructure programme carrying high-sensitivity engineering and commercial data), roughly 138,800 paths sit in HR directories, about 86,300 under general project working data, ~38,700 in compliance records, ~27,800 in invoicing/financial material, plus payroll (~11,150), tax/TDS (~4,275), salary (~4,670), insurance (~1,470) and identity data such as Aadhaar/PAN (~2,240) and passport (~148). A further ~105,750 paths are OS-level backup spillage under AppData, browser-artifact and token-container locations swept up by backing up entire user profiles wholesale.   By type the set is dominated by PDF (~325,200), Excel (~115,000), Word (~76,900), CAD drawings (~20,100), archives (~24,500) and images (~66,500).   The assessment: finance, HR, compliance, contracts and engineering IP -including critical infrastructure project files  are exposed, enabling targeted retrieval, identity/PII exposure and spear-phishing target selection, with root causes pointing to over-broad backup scope, a reachable staging area, and weak retention controls  Tata Electronics   The artifact is a full recursive file-system inventory spanning four internal departmental file servers, enumerating 204,341 file paths one per line, split across a QA share (~111,600 paths, more than half), an NPI/new-product share (~74,200), an HR share (~11,100) and a general file server (~7,400), each subdivided by drive.   Unlike the document-heavy Reliance set, this exposure is manufacturing- and engineering-IP-heavy: about 63,800 paths relate to board-level (MLB) data and ~53,300 to final assembly/test (FATP), with AOI/vision data (~13,600), equipment data (~3,700), burn-in (~1,820), functional test (~720) and repair (~1,000) all present, alongside ~11,100 HR-share paths and codenamed product/program references appearing tens of thousands of times across the set (unreleased-product and test-program identifiers). By type it is led by inspection/AOI images (~43,000 jpg), Excel (~27,700), PDF (~26,300), CSV (~13,100), and a large body of logs and test data (txt/json/db/log totalling 21,000+), plus station/program configuration files and libraries (~8,300 dll).   The assessment: the crown jewels here are process and product data - test programs, station configurations, yield/inspection data and codenamed product references directly useful for competitive intelligence and process reconstruction, with some OT-adjacent artifacts (equipment and station configuration) raising additional concern and the HR share adding personnel-data and social-engineering risk.

In 2017 I left a job, and within a week one of my former employees shut down and physically threw away a system I had set up for some QOL improvements for employees. It was very clearly documented (I've written technical documentation books).  It was a dashboard that showed traffic around the office, weather, if there was a ballgame at the ballpark literally next door (building parking got used for ball games), alerts from IT, live IT system statuses, and links to the ticket system, knowledge base, and employee handbook. It was accessible online or by looking at it on a display I put up on a wall for folks. People absolutely loved it.  My former number two literally took the display down and threw the physical server into the trash. A couple folks texted me asking why I took it down before I left, I said I didn't. They sent me a picture of it in the e-waste bin. I messaged him asking why, he said, he "always hated how transparent it was what we were working on."   He was not picked to be my replacement. The new guy who came in undid more things and then they got nailed in a $200k invoice scam and then he was fired. The company is gone now. :(
FYI driving over a sprinkler absolutely will fuck it up don't do that
Protip to bug hunters: Look for error conditions that throw strings/pages never designed for consumer consumption and lacking in localization.

Jace pulled out a laptop with ODB, and I knew he was going to kill himself. My engine coolant thermistor pulled low, firing powertrain limp DTC, opening diagnostic conceits on the CAN gateway.  He was going to kill me, too.  The Dying Breed  a short story by SwiftOnSecurity
Peasants in 1826: "Capitalists aren't putting enough ingredients in my food!"  Peasants in 2026: "Capitalists are putting too many ingredients in my food!"  You will NEVER make these people happy.

When I left that company, I had to tear down several of the things I built because I had the experience knowing what it felt like encountering my own systems, and also that things would break catastrophically and they would have no idea how to maintain it. That was a sad week.
Me, who has only listened to Taylor Swift, putting a Michael Jackson record on:  "Wow, you can definitely hear the influence of Taylor Swift."

One of the most privileged perspective things I have in my career, is working at a place for 10 years. Instead of blaming a rando doing something years ago, increasingly it was me.  A younger, naïve, idealistic me who thought some other sucker would be maintaining this by now.

llm_summary:
🤖 [GEMINI 분석] 2026-06-25 19:47 KST
🟠 위협 점수: 8/10 (높음)

제공해주신 위협 인텔리전스 데이터를 분석한 결과입니다.

### 1. 현재 주요 사이버보안 트렌드
최근 공격자들은 AI 에이전트 마켓플레이스(ClawHub 등)를 악용하여 악성 'Skill'을 배포하는 등 AI 생태계의 공급망 공격을 본격화하고 있습니다. 또한, macOS 환경을 타겟으로 한 Rust 기반의 Gaslight 악성코드가 AI 분석 도구를 우회하기 위해 Prompt Injection 기법을 사용하는 등 방어 체계의 자동화 분석을 무력화하려는 시도가 관찰됩니다. Cisco SD-WAN과 같은 네트워크 인프라 취약점(Zero-day)을 활용한 Root 권한 탈취 및 설정 변경 사례가 지속적으로 보고되고 있어, 인프라 보안 패치와 더불어 탐지 우회 기법에 대한 모니터링이 필수적입니다.

### 2. 주요 위협 행위자 / 캠페인
*   **Scattered Spider**: 런던 교통국(TfL) 공격 혐의로 주요 조직원들이 유죄를 인정함.
*   **The Gentlemen**: EDR Killer 프레임워크를 유지하며 랜섬웨어 공격을 수행하는 RaaS 그룹.
*   **Kimsuky**: 개인정보 유출을 사칭한 스피어 피싱 공격을 통해 국내 보안 담당자를 타겟팅.
*   **ShinyEnigma (Millenium RAT 개발자)**: 저비용 MaaS 모델을 통해 광범위한 악성코드 접근성을 제공.

### 3. 한국 관련 위협
*   **KISA**: 협력업체 간담회를 통해 계약제도 개선 및 상생 협력 논의(데일리시큐).
*   **SGA솔루션즈**: 레드캐슬 V6.0 CC인증 획득 및 AI 서버 보안 시장 공략(데일리시큐).
*   **Kimsuky**: 국내 보안 담당자를 대상으로 한 스피어 피싱 공격 지속 확인(이스트시큐리티).

### 4. 주요 CVE / 취약점
*   **CVE-2026-48907**: Joomla JCE, CVSS 10.0, 적극적 악용 중.
*   **CVE-2026-34908/34909/34910**: Ubiquity UniFiOS, CVSS 10.0, 적극적 악용 중.
*   **CVE-2026-20245**: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN, 고위험(Root Access), Zero-day 악용 확인.
*   **CVE-2026-45504**: Microsoft Exchange, CVSS 8.8, PoC 공개.
*   **CVE-2026-20230**: Cisco Unified CM, CVSS 8.6, SSRF 취약점 악용 확인.

### 5. IOC 요약
*   **CVE**:
    *   CVE-2026-20245 ([TheHackerNews](https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/cisco-catalyst-sd-wan-zero-day.html))
    *   CVE-2026-48907 ([CCBalert](https://twitter.com/CCBalert/status/2070093624583332085))
    *   CVE-2026-34908/34909/34910 ([CCBalert](https://twitter.com/CCBalert/status/2070089553826717765))
    *   CVE-2026-20230 ([CCBalert](https://twitter.com/CCBalert/status/2070092728382226757))
    *   CVE-2026-45504 ([DarkWebInformer](https://twitter.com/DarkWebInformer/status/2069854919859765699))

### 6. 즉각 대응 권고
*   **인프라 패치**: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN 및 UniFiOS 장비의 최신 보안 패치를 즉시 적용하고, 관리자 계정의 비정상적인 생성 여부를 감사하십시오.
*   **AI 도구 보안**: AI 에이전트 마켓플레이스에서 다운로드한 'Skill'이나 플러그인 사용을 제한하고, 코드 실행 환경에서의 권한 격리(Sandboxing)를 강화하십시오.
*   **피싱 대응**: 보안 담당자를 타겟으로 한 '개인정보 유출' 사칭 메일이 유포 중이므로, 첨부파일 실행 전 샌드박스 분석 및 발신자 검증을 철저히 수행하십시오.

### 7. 위협 수준 평가
*   **점수**: 8/10
*   **근거**: 인프라 장비(Cisco, Ubiquity)의 Zero-day 악용이 활발하고, AI 에이전트를 통한 새로운 형태의 공급망 공격이 확인되어 기업 보안 환경에 높은 위험을 초래함.
